1: <header>Effects of swine manure storage time on solid-liquid separation and biogas production: A life-cycle assessment approach </header>
C.E. Hollas A.C. Bolsan , A. Chini B. Venturin a , G. Bonassa ² , D. Cândidos, F.G. Antes , R.L. R. Steinmetz N.V. Prado A. Kunz a,c,d,*

<list>a UNIOESTE/CASCAVEL/CCET/PGEAGRI, Western Paraná State University, 85819-110, Cascavel, PR, Brazil 
b Universidade Tecnológica Federal Do Parana, 85660-000, Dois Vizinhos, PR, Brazil 
C Universidade Federal da Fronteira Sul, 99700-000, Erechim, RS, Brazil

ARTICLEINF|ABSTRACT Keywords: Animal manure Anaerobic digestion Settling Screening Waste management Energy recovery Circular economy|In the present study was evaluated and compared the effects of swine manure storage on a low-cost solid-liquid separation approach. We also determined the consequences of these stages on further treatment for energy re- covery and environmental impact reduction. Different hydraulic retention times were studied in the solid-liquid separation techniques to determine the best pre-treatment conditions and apply these conditions in anaerobic digestion. This evaluation highlighted the possibility and advantages of applying solid-liquid separation using a settling pre-treatment in the swine chain. Storage time significantly affected the efficiency of screening which was reduced by 80 + 0.5% after 5 d compared to fresh manure screening. This directly reflected the sludge volume generated in the following stages, with sludge increments of 51 I 0.1% for settling. The waste solid- liquid separation provided better energy production results when the manure storage time was less than 5 d, resulting in improved power generation capacity of the system (up to 4.5 kWh of electricity for each m³ of raw manure). We observed the positive contribution of adequate manure management in swine sustainability through the life-cycle assessment of the swine chain during manure storage, solid-liquid separation, and anaerobic digestion. It promoted low greenhouse gas emissions, resulted in a carbon-neutral chain, and maxi- mized energy production and recovery. The results highlighted the importance of previous solid-liquid separa- tion in anaerobic digestion and the relevance of proper effluent management because more than 5 d of storage time compromised subsequent processes. This was also observed during the life-cycle assessment.
2: Increasing demand for animal protein has prompted at shift in swine production from small family-owned farms to larger industrialized fa- cilities, such as confined animal feeding operations (CAFOs), worldwide [1,2]. With such intensification, large quantities of manure are gener- ated, limiting the worldwide continuous agronomic use of agricultural waste as a fertilizer. Unfortunately, constant concerns regarding soil, water, and air contamination have required more sustainable systems and better management and technological strategies for its residues [3, 4].
The mitigation of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions combined with reducing fossil fuel dependence and searching for alternative energy

sources have become a global challenge. Despite constant technological evolution, which makes the production processes more sustainable, the search for a sustainable energy matrix and reducing GHG remain a global problem [5-8]. Swine production has contributed to GHG emis- sions (i.e., carbon dioxide [CO2], nitrous oxide [N2O] and methane [CH4]), and inadequate waste management is one of the main causes [9, 10]. Although these gases are emitted during all swine production phases, storage is considered the critical stage of the process because it can change waste constituents and promote gas emission because of degradation [11,12].

To mitigate these problems, currently several technologies are currently available to manage swine effluents, among which anaerobic digestion (AD) is one of the most attractive alternatives for the treatment of swine wastewater fractions [13,14]. Hence, swine manure has been

explored as a potential renewable energy source to replace fossil fuels, contributing to energy matrix diversification in several countries [15, 16]. This management enables energy recovery, promotes a circular economy, and mitigates GHG emissions of the swine production chain [17,18].

In recent years, the improvement of treatment technologies under several conditions and wastewater pre-treatment has been the focus of many studies. Because of the solid content of swine manure, solid-liquid separation (SLS) is a widespread pre-treatment applied to these effluents before to biological processes (except for high solid rate biodigesters) [19-21]. SLS as a pre-treatment for AD can be used for process opti- mization, increasing the biogas/methane yield, because of better use of the highly biodegradable fraction (liquid fraction), especially in covered lagoon biodigesters (BLC). Additionally, it can also reduce costs and operational problems in the treatment system, such as siltation and its subsequent process overload because of solid deposition inside the reactor, reducing the hydraulic retention time [22-28].

SLS can be performed using a distinct range of technologies. Among the commonly applied strategies for swine manure pre-treatment, screening, settling, and chemical flocculation, are commercial methods [29-31]. Despite the established efficiency of these pre-treatments, their costs remain a limiting factor in swine wastewater management [32,33]. Thus, settling has become an attractive alterna- tive because of its low-cost and simple operation [23,34]. However, once organic manure matter easily degrades and suspended particles destabilize, the biomass storage time should be as short as possible to avoid compromising the efficiency of SLS technologies, and subse- quently, the AD [21,22]. Reduced manure storage time also minimizes GHG emissions and improves energy recovery [8,35].

is a good approach to compare and measure the environmental impact of biogas production from swine manure and ensure its sustainability as an energy resource. The LCA considers all inputs and outputs of materials and energy flow during all stages, examining and promoting information related to the sustainability of swine production and manure treatment, which can be positive or negative [13,36]. The LCA is defined according to ISO 14040-14044:2006 [37,38] as the "compilation and evaluation of the inputs, outputs and potential environmental impacts of a product system throughout its life cycle". Such an evaluation is an effective tool for assessing and solving environmental problems from the biogas pro- duction based on a wide range of feedstocks and wastes [39]. According to Tabatabaei et al. [40], in regard to different types of biofuels present in the renewable matrix, biogas from organic wastes in AD is a promising alternative energy source with which to solve a portion of global energy and environmental challenges. Despite these benefits, they can also be produced from different types of organic wastes. Many studies have addressed the LCA for biogas production [41]. In Table 1, some studies regarding biogas production using the LCA approach are presented for different biomasses and wastes.

Although SLS and AD can manage the waste from swine production to solve environmental issues and, supply an energy demand, the appropriation of this route, and sustainability in relation to environ- mental impacts (i.e., GHG emissions) must be carefully analyzed. Therefore, the life-cycle assessment (LCA) standardization methodology

According to Table 1, the type of pre-treatment applied to the wastes can play different roles in biogas yield, and consequently, LCA. Addi- tionally, the importance of LCA analysis has been observed in different production chains. Several strategies has been proposed to increase AD efficiency, and the best is based on biomass characteristics (i.g., waste biodegradability) [59]. Consequently, this significantly improves the environmental and economic feasibility of biogas production plants by reducing capital and operating expenses [60].

Until now, most studies have determined the impacts of animal manure pre-treatment on AD yield and LCA. Although some reports also determined the impact of manure storage time in the LCA because of CH4 and N2O emissions [41], there is little information that correlates the influence of manure storage time on SLS efficiency and the combined global effect on the swine LCA. This knowledge provides a solid strategy to improve the efficiency of biogas production and energy recovery of

Several strategies has been proposed to increase AD efficiency, and the best is based on biomass characteristics (i.g., waste biodegradability) [59]. Consequently, this significantly improves the environmental and economic feasibility of biogas production plants by reducing capital and operating expenses [60].

Until now, most studies have determined the impacts of animal manure pre-treatment on AD yield and LCA. Although some reports also determined the impact of manure storage time in the LCA because of CH4 and N2O emissions [41], there is little information that correlates the influence of manure storage time on SLS efficiency and the combined global effect on the swine LCA. This knowledge provides a solid strategy to improve the efficiency of biogas production and energy recovery of
6: Increasing demand for animal protein has prompted at shift in swine production from small family-owned farms to larger industrialized fa- cilities, such as confined animal feeding operations (CAFOs), worldwide [1,2]. With such intensification, large quantities of manure are gener- ated, limiting the worldwide continuous agronomic use of agricultural waste as a fertilizer. Unfortunately, constant concerns regarding soil, water, and air contamination have required more sustainable systems and better management and technological strategies for its residues [3, 4].
The mitigation of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions combined with reducing fossil fuel dependence and searching for alternative energy

sources have become a global challenge. Despite constant technological evolution, which makes the production processes more sustainable, the search for a sustainable energy matrix and reducing GHG remain a global problem [5-8]. Swine production has contributed to GHG emis- sions (i.e., carbon dioxide [CO2], nitrous oxide [N2O] and methane [CH4]), and inadequate waste management is one of the main causes [9, 10]. Although these gases are emitted during all swine production phases, storage is considered the critical stage of the process because it can change waste constituents and promote gas emission because of degradation [11,12].

To mitigate these problems, currently several technologies are currently available to manage swine effluents, among which anaerobic digestion (AD) is one of the most attractive alternatives for the treatment of swine wastewater fractions [13,14]. Hence, swine manure has been

explored as a potential renewable energy source to replace fossil fuels, contributing to energy matrix diversification in several countries [15, 16]. This management enables energy recovery, promotes a circular economy, and mitigates GHG emissions of the swine production chain [17,18].

In recent years, the improvement of treatment technologies under several conditions and wastewater pre-treatment has been the focus of many studies. Because of the solid content of swine manure, solid-liquid separation (SLS) is a widespread pre-treatment applied to these effluents before to biological processes (except for high solid rate biodigesters) [19-21]. SLS as a pre-treatment for AD can be used for process opti- mization, increasing the biogas/methane yield, because of better use of the highly biodegradable fraction (liquid fraction), especially in covered lagoon biodigesters (BLC). Additionally, it can also reduce costs and operational problems in the treatment system, such as siltation and its subsequent process overload because of solid deposition inside the reactor, reducing the hydraulic retention time [22-28].

SLS can be performed using a distinct range of technologies. Among the commonly applied strategies for swine manure pre-treatment, screening, settling, and chemical flocculation, are commercial methods [29-31]. Despite the established efficiency of these pre-treatments, their costs remain a limiting factor in swine wastewater management [32,33]. Thus, settling has become an attractive alterna- tive because of its low-cost and simple operation [23,34]. However, once organic manure matter easily degrades and suspended particles destabilize, the biomass storage time should be as short as possible to avoid compromising the efficiency of SLS technologies, and subse- quently, the AD [21,22]. Reduced manure storage time also minimizes GHG emissions and improves energy recovery [8,35].

is a good approach to compare and measure the environmental impact of biogas production from swine manure and ensure its sustainability as an energy resource. The LCA considers all inputs and outputs of materials and energy flow during all stages, examining and promoting information related to the sustainability of swine production and manure treatment, which can be positive or negative [13,36]. The LCA is defined according to ISO 14040-14044:2006 [37,38] as the "compilation and evaluation of the inputs, outputs and potential environmental impacts of a product system throughout its life cycle". Such an evaluation is an effective tool for assessing and solving environmental problems from the biogas pro- duction based on a wide range of feedstocks and wastes [39]. According to Tabatabaei et al. [40], in regard to different types of biofuels present in the renewable matrix, biogas from organic wastes in AD is a promising alternative energy source with which to solve a portion of global energy and environmental challenges. Despite these benefits, they can also be produced from different types of organic wastes. Many studies have addressed the LCA for biogas production [41]. In Table 1, some studies regarding biogas production using the LCA approach are presented for different biomasses and wastes.

Although SLS and AD can manage the waste from swine production to solve environmental issues and, supply an energy demand, the appropriation of this route, and sustainability in relation to environ- mental impacts (i.e., GHG emissions) must be carefully analyzed. Although these gases are emitted during all swine production phases, storage is considered the critical stage of the process because it can change waste constituents and promote gas emission because of degradation [11,12].

To mitigate these problems, currently several technologies are currently available to manage swine effluents, among which anaerobic digestion (AD) is one of the most attractive alternatives for the treatment of swine wastewater fractions [13,14]. Hence, swine manure has been

explored as a potential renewable energy source to replace fossil fuels, contributing to energy matrix diversification in several countries [15, 16]. This management enables energy recovery, promotes a circular economy, and mitigates GHG emissions of the swine production chain [17,18].

In recent years, the improvement of treatment technologies under several conditions and wastewater pre-treatment has been the focus of many studies. Because of the solid content of swine manure, solid-liquid separation (SLS) is a widespread pre-treatment applied to these effluents before to biological processes (except for high solid rate biodigesters) [19-21]. SLS as a pre-treatment for AD can be used for process opti- mization, increasing the biogas/methane yield, because of better use of the highly biodegradable fraction (liquid fraction), especially in covered lagoon biodigesters (BLC). Additionally, it can also reduce costs and operational problems in the treatment system, such as siltation and its subsequent process overload because of solid deposition inside the reactor, reducing the hydraulic retention time [22-28].

SLS can be performed using a distinct range of technologies. Among the commonly applied strategies for swine manure pre-treatment, screening, settling, and chemical flocculation, are commercial methods [29-31]. Despite the established efficiency of these pre-treatments, their costs remain a limiting factor in swine wastewater management [32,33]. Thus, settling has become an attractive alterna- tive because of its low-cost and simple operation [23,34]. However, once organic manure matter easily degrades and suspended particles destabilize, the biomass storage time should be as short as possible to avoid compromising the efficiency of SLS technologies, and subse- quently, the AD [21,22]. Reduced manure storage time also minimizes GHG emissions and improves energy recovery [8,35].

is a good approach to compare and measure the environmental impact of biogas production from swine manure and ensure its sustainability as an energy resource. The LCA considers all inputs and outputs of materials and energy flow during all stages, examining and promoting information related to the sustainability of swine production and manure treatment, which can be positive or negative [13,36]. The LCA is defined according to ISO 14040-14044:2006 [37,38] as the "compilation and evaluation of the inputs, outputs and potential environmental impacts of a product system throughout its life cycle". Such an evaluation is an effective tool for assessing and solving environmental problems from the biogas pro- duction based on a wide range of feedstocks and wastes [39]. According to Tabatabaei et al. [40], in regard to different types of biofuels present in the renewable matrix, biogas from organic wastes in AD is a promising alternative energy source with which to solve a portion of global energy and environmental challenges. Despite these benefits, they can also be produced from different types of organic wastes. Many studies have addressed the LCA for biogas production [41]. In Table 1, some studies regarding biogas production using the LCA approach are presented for different biomasses and wastes.

Although SLS and AD can manage the waste from swine production to solve environmental issues and, supply an energy demand, the appropriation of this route, and sustainability in relation to environ- mental impacts (i.e., GHG emissions) must be carefully analyzed. Therefore, the life-cycle assessment (LCA) standardization methodology

According to Table 1, the type of pre-treatment applied to the wastes can play different roles in biogas yield, and consequently, LCA. Addi- tionally, the importance of LCA analysis has been observed in different production chains. Several strategies has been proposed to increase AD efficiency, and the best is based on biomass characteristics (i.g., waste biodegradability) [59]. Consequently, this significantly improves the environmental and economic feasibility of biogas production plants by reducing capital and operating expenses [60].

Until now, most studies have determined the impacts of animal manure pre-treatment on AD yield and LCA. Although some reports also determined the impact of manure storage time in the LCA because of CH4 and N2O emissions [41], there is little information that correlates the influence of manure storage time on SLS efficiency and the combined global effect on the swine LCA. This knowledge provides a solid strategy to improve the efficiency of biogas production and energy recovery of
11: Application of LCA approach on different biomass and wastes for biogas production.

Biomass|Main considerations|Reference Animal manure|directly influenced by the entire treatment of the farm. AD was evaluated as an alternative to manage the wastes from dairy farm, with the LCA approach; Then, the environmental categories of global warming potential, acidification, eutrophication and photochemical ozone creation were assessed, which promoted reductions in the environmental impact of 25.7, 49.5, 18.1 and 16.1, respectively; Besides, the manure accumulation and storage time on the sand bed is one of the highest contributors to global warming and photochemical ozone potential, due to excessive emissions of CH4. With such, there are also losses on biogas production.|[52] Microalgae|The advantages of microalgae AD are due that this biomass fast grow in non-arable land areas and accumulates a high quantity of digestible macromolecules in the biomass, for biogas production; During the microalgae growing, it reduces CO2 from air to its cultivation, which also does not require fresh water and nutrients, impacting the LCA positively.|[53] Lignocellulosic biomass|To apply these kinds of wastes, some kind of pretreatment before the AD is required (i.e. sulfuric acid (SA), sodium hydroxide (SH), aqueous ammonia (AA), and sodium carbonate (SC)); These pretreatments affect biogas yield and consequently the LCA.|[54] Municipal solid waste|Manage gas from AD as vehicle applications promote better environmental issues than as energy, according with the LCA results; The advantages related with the utilization of methane as road transport fuel in substitution of fossil fuels is about the minimization of greenhouse gases emission, more than if it was applied as an energy source.|[55] Sludge from wastewater treatment plant|The AD was evaluated as an alternative to organic matter treatment of sludges from WWTP, to evaluate the consequently the environmental impacts according with the LCA; Low CO2 emissions were observed with the implementation of this management.|[56] Food waste|The palm oil waste was evaluated as a source to biogas production by AD; According with the LCA, from the point of view of global warming, land use and water consumption, this a promising technology that can be used to meet the goals for a sustainable energy matrix; The sustainability of biogas production can be enhanced with better techniques of biogas production (i.e. pretreatment routes), utilization and digestate management.|[57] Vinasse|The LCA of vinasse AD were analyzed to compare the environmental impact of this system with open lagoons; According, 77% of the impact was reduced with the AD management, with the biogas production and recovery, reducing the negative environmental impact of this category.|[58]

Biomass|Main considerations|Reference Food waste|Biogas production from food wastes is more suitable than other biological alternatives from LCA.|[42] Food waste|Co-digestion of food waste (nappies and expired food wastes) were assessed by LCA; The tested approach showed a positive environmental impact: lower emissions of CO2 were obtained with less human damage if compared with landfilling and incineration of the wastes.|[43] Food waste|Biogas production from co-digestion of food waste with corn silage; According to the LCA, this is an alternative to the reduction of 42% in the carbon footprint of the electricity produced.|[44] Municipal solid waste|Biogas production from co-digestion of municipal solid waste with sewage sludge as an alternative to incineration, composting and landfilling; The co- digestion showed lower environmental impact, with advantages in relation to the LCA (global warming potential, acidification, eutrophication and ecotoxicity); The benefits were also related with environmental and economic sustainability.|[45] Food waste|Integration of pyrolysis with anaerobic digestion were compared, according with the LCA; Eutrophication can be reduced around 64% and acidification 51%, with food waste management;Biochar and biogas are generated as alternative energy sources.|[46] Animal manure (effluent slurry)|Tests with digestion of cow-dung and co- digestion of cow-dung with potato-pulp; The proportions of manure-pulp mixtures impact the LCA categories (i.e. climate change, eutrophication, and acidification potential); The evaluation demonstrates the importance and relevance of these types of assessments.|[47] Animal manure|Some kind of pretreatment is necessary to increase the yield of biogas from manure, due to it high solids content; Pretreatments approaches also impacts the LCA of animal manure AD and biogas production: it increases the sustainability of the chain, through better waste use.|[48] Animal manure|The management of poultry litter by AD and biogas production leads to reduced environmental impacts of this waste, terrestrial acidification, and eutrophication. According to the LCA, the AD of this feedstock conducts valorization pathways in the circular economy and reduces environmental impacts.|[49] Animal manure and municipal organic waste|In relation to fossil diesel, gains around 93-131% in greenhouse gases emissions were achieved with the management of the wastes by AD, according with the LCA; Acidification and territorial eutrophication impacts were also reduced (257 and 700%, respectively); Fertilizer and nutrient recovery techniques with the digestate from the AD would promote an ever-great sustainability to the chain.|[50] Animal manure|Pretreatment of dairy and swine manure would improve the efficiency of the AD, due to the fibrous content of these wastes; The management of wastes, pretreatment and AD efficiency affects the global LCA, since the environmental performance is|[51]
the swine production system, creating adjustments that reduce its environmental impact.

Accordingly, research is needed that addresses certain issues and presents a systematic and reliable assessment of the LCA, considering the SLS and manure storage time. In this study, we (i) determined the in- fluence of swine manure storage time on SLS efficiency (based on screening, settling, and chemical flocculation); (ii) explored the influ- ence of SLS on swine biogas and methane yield; and (iii) conducted an LCA to evaluate the environmental impact of biogas production during different scenarios, including variation in manure storage time and SLS.

The co- digestion showed lower environmental impact, with advantages in relation to the LCA (global warming potential, acidification, eutrophication and ecotoxicity); The benefits were also related with environmental and economic sustainability.|[45] Food waste|Integration of pyrolysis with anaerobic digestion were compared, according with the LCA; Eutrophication can be reduced around 64% and acidification 51%, with food waste management;Biochar and biogas are generated as alternative energy sources.|[46] Animal manure (effluent slurry)|Tests with digestion of cow-dung and co- digestion of cow-dung with potato-pulp; The proportions of manure-pulp mixtures impact the LCA categories (i.e. climate change, eutrophication, and acidification potential); The evaluation demonstrates the importance and relevance of these types of assessments.|[47] Animal manure|Some kind of pretreatment is necessary to increase the yield of biogas from manure, due to it high solids content; Pretreatments approaches also impacts the LCA of animal manure AD and biogas production: it increases the sustainability of the chain, through better waste use.|[48] Animal manure|The management of poultry litter by AD and biogas production leads to reduced environmental impacts of this waste, terrestrial acidification, and eutrophication. According to the LCA, the AD of this feedstock conducts valorization pathways in the circular economy and reduces environmental impacts.|[49] Animal manure and municipal organic waste|In relation to fossil diesel, gains around 93-131% in greenhouse gases emissions were achieved with the management of the wastes by AD, according with the LCA; Acidification and territorial eutrophication impacts were also reduced (257 and 700%, respectively); Fertilizer and nutrient recovery techniques with the digestate from the AD would promote an ever-great sustainability to the chain.|[50] Animal manure|Pretreatment of dairy and swine manure would improve the efficiency of the AD, due to the fibrous content of these wastes; The management of wastes, pretreatment and AD efficiency affects the global LCA, since the environmental performance is|[51]
the swine production system, creating adjustments that reduce its environmental impact.

Accordingly, research is needed that addresses certain issues and presents a systematic and reliable assessment of the LCA, considering the SLS and manure storage time. In this study, we (i) determined the in- fluence of swine manure storage time on SLS efficiency (based on screening, settling, and chemical flocculation); (ii) explored the influ- ence of SLS on swine biogas and methane yield; and (iii) conducted an LCA to evaluate the environmental impact of biogas production during different scenarios, including variation in manure storage time and SLS.
16: A representative fresh raw swine manure (M1) sample (200 L) was collected directly from the reception pits inside a gestating SOW house (previously cleaned 1 d before sample collection) in the experimental swine production system at the National Swine and Poultry Research Center (Embrapa Suínos e Aves) located in Concórdia, Santa Catarina, Brazil (27°18'S, 51°59'W). The manure was stored in a dark poly- ethylene container at room temperature (25 I 3 °C).
For the biochemical methane potential (BMP) experiments, raw swine manure (M2) sample was collected after 3 d of storage in recep- tion pits inside a gestating SOW house.
17: The effects of storage time on the SLS of raw manure (at drainage channels) were determined using simplified and low-cost separation strategies based on screening and settling and compared with chemical flocculation. The experiments were performed for 20 d. During this period, samples were collected daily and submitted to an initial SLS by screening using a 2 mm sieve for coarse particle separation. The resulting solids retained in the sieve (SSF1) were characterized, and the liquid fraction (SLF1) was subjected to another SLS using settling or chemical flocculation techniques (Fig. 1). Moreover, the supernatant from the settling was subjected to settling tests for 60 min in an Imhoff cone. All tests were performed in triplicate.
18: The experimental apparatus used for SLS experiments was composed of (i) a feed tank continuously stirred by a mechanical stirrer (RW20 D S32, Ika); (ii) two peristaltic pumps (07554-95, Master Flex and BP-600, Milan) for system feeding; and (iii) a conical decanter with a working volume of L. The decanter operated with continuous feeding at the top, intermittent disposal of sludge at the bottom (10 1 3% of the feed vol- ume), and 30 min of hydraulic retention time (HRT).

To monitor the effects of manure storage time on SLS, the solid (StSF1) and liquid fractions (StLF1) from the tests were characterized daily according to the physicochemical properties of the solid and liquid fractions generated during the SLS experiment.
19: SLS tests with chemical additives were performed by chemical floc- culation, using as coagulant a 10% (v v-1 aqueous solution of poly- phenolic organic polymer (liquid form containing 30% tannic acid (TA) and 0.01%, wv-1, Veta Organic®, Brazilian Wattle Extracts, Brazil) and an aqueous solution of polyacrylamide (PAM) (granular form, Activator QR, Brazilian Wattle Extracts, Brazil). Both coagulants were prepared immediately before use by dilution with water under vigorous stirring for 15 min.

These tests were conducted using a Jar-Test (JT102/3, Milan, Colombo, Brazil) with a working volume of L, with the following steps: (i) the influent was stirred for 5 min at 200 rpm with 75 mL of TA (10% v-1), (ii) 15 mL of PAM (0.01% W was added to the system at 50 rpm for 10 min, and finally (iii) the mixture was maintained without mixing for 15 min for solid fraction settling [61]. Both fractions (chemical flocculation solid fraction (CfSF1) and chemical flocculation liquid fraction (CfLF1)) were characterized using physicochemical analyses.
20: The BMP was determined using raw swine manure and each of the fractions from the SLS: solid retained in the sieve (SSF2), supernatant from screening (SLF2), settled sludge (StSF30, StSF 10, and StSF5), and supernatant from settling (StLF30, StLF10, and StLF5). Samples of su- pernatant from settling and settled sludge were collected at three HRT (5, 10, and 30 min).
AD experiments were performed to determine the BMP, in batches
Fig. 1. Schematic representation of swine manure solid-liquid separation tests for screening and subsequent settling or chemical flocculation.

and triplicate, under mesophilic temperature conditions (37 °C) ac- cording to VDI 4630 [62]. For all SLS fractions and raw manure, the tests were conducted using a 250 mL reactor flask, and the gas volume was measured using eudiometer graduated tubes (500 mL). The mesophilic anaerobic inoculum was prepared as described by Steinmetz et al. [63]. The gas production was monitored daily by displacement of the column of liquid sealant [64] in the eudiometer tube, and the dried biogas volume was corrected to 273.15 K and 1013 hPa. The test was consid- ered stabilized and finalized when the daily biogas production was less than or equal to 1% of the total volume [62].
For the biogas composition evaluation (CH4), the samples were analyzed by infrared and electrochemical sensors on a BIOGAS 5000 (Landtec, Dexter, USA).

Samples of su- pernatant from settling and settled sludge were collected at three HRT (5, 10, and 30 min).

22: All analytical determinations were performed at least in triplicates, and the results represent the average and standard deviation. Total ammonia nitrogen (TAN: NH4-N + NH3-N, expressed as NH3-N) was measured by a colorimetric method in a flow injection analysis system (model 2500, Fialab Instruments, Seattle, USA), based on the method Rice et al. [65], as described by Hollas et al. [66]. Total solids (TS), fixed solids (FS), volatile solids (VS), total suspended solids (TSS), volatile suspended solids (VSS), and fixed suspended solids (FSS) were quanti- fied gravimetrically according to standard methods [65].

The soluble total organic carbon (TOC) and total nitrogen (TN) were determined using a TOC analyzer (TOC-LCPH/CPN, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) following the manufacturer's recommendations. A the relative standard deviation of less than 2.5% was obtained in triplicate to the determine of TOC and TN. Total phosphorous (TP) was quantified by the molibdovanadato colorimetric method using a UV-VIS spectrophotom- eter (model Cary 50, Agilent, Santa Clara, USA). Potassium was quan- tified by flame photometry (B-462, Micronal, Santo André, Brazil) [65].

Total phosphorous (TP) was quantified by the molibdovanadato colorimetric method using a UV-VIS spectrophotometer (model Cary 50, Agilent, Santa Clara, USA). Potassium was quantified by flame photometry (B-462, Micronal, Santo André, Brazil) [65]

27: The LCA was applied to evaluate the environmental aspects and possible impacts associated with swine manure storage time and energy use by AD. The evaluation was performed according to the International Organization for Standardization (ISO14040 and 14,044) provided for any LCA study [37,38]. The software OpenLCA 1.10.2 [73] was used to perform the evaluation, (free open-source software), with detailed in- sights regarding the results and analysis performed, allowing for greater reliability and transparency.
The LCA approach was split into three scenarios to compare the

Swine raw manure Scenario 1||Anaerobic Digestion in Covered Lagoon (CLB) (Rec 60%)|Biogas|Electric energy potential|| Swine raw manure Scenario 2|Solid-Liquid Separation (screening and settling,30 min of HRT)|Liquid Fraction Solid Fraction|Anaerobic Digestion in CLB (Rec 95%) Anaerobic Digestion in CSTR (Rec 50%)|Biogas|Electric energy potential| Swine raw manure Scenario 3|Solid-Liquid Separation (screening and settling, 5 min of HRT)|Liquid Fraction Solid Fraction|Anaerobic Digestion in CLB (Rec 67%) Anaerobic Digestion in CSTR (Rec 50%)|Biogas|Electric energy potential|
Fig. 2. Hypothetical scenarios to estimate the energy potential with or without use of solid-liquid separation.

environmental profiles of swine manure treatment systems (Fig. 3). The of scenarios included manure production and storage in swine produc- tion installations; manure organic matter stabilization followed by field application and/or biogas production, or electricity and heat cogene- ration at the combined heat and power (CHP) plant; AD digestate stor- age and application as fertilizer.

The baseline was comprised of the storage of waste in the in- stallations (~8d) [74], followed by stabilization in an open storage tank (120 d) [75], transportation to the field, and soil application as a fer- tilizer source (N, P, and K). Scenario 1 included manure storage in the installation (8 d), followed by organic matter degradation by AD (30 d), and storage of digestate in an open storage tank (60 d). Then, the digestate was applied as fertilizer in the soil (after 60 d of storage, a hot spot for emissions in LCA studies of biogas systems), and the gaseous fraction (biogas) could be used for electricity and heat production. In Scenario 2, the swine manure storage in the installations was reduced to 3 d. The effluent was pretreated by before AD of both fractions. The biological process occurred in separate reactors because of to the con- centration of manure solids. As in Scenario 1, the liquid fraction was also used as a fertilizer and gaseous product for heat and electricity pro- duction (Fig. 3). For all scenarios, the impacts related to the construction steps of the physical installations were not included in the assessment, because they were not a result of changes in manure management, and the objective was to verify the impacts associated with effluent storage time and biogas production.

The LCA modeling for the current study was projected for the treatment of 1 m³ of swine manure. The life-cycle inventory (LCI) was constructed to understand the magnitude and significance of the po- tential environmental impacts of the effluents. LCI considered the pro- cess involved (and corresponding inputs and outputs) and the emissions and energetic demands from each phase. To understand and describe the consequences of a decision (Baseline, Scenario 1 or 2) for the environ- ment, we used some marginal data from the Ecoinvent 3.6 [76] data- base, data estimation based on the relevant literature, the inventory described by Cherubini et al. [77] and the experiments developed in present the study (Table More detailed information on the inventory estimates is provided in the Supplementary Material.

IMPACT World+, a globally regionalized life-cycle impact assess- ment method, was used to evaluate the described systems (Table 2). The method integrates Latin America and relates spatial variability to pri- ority elementary flows in a regionalized way [84]. Because the scenario in this study refers to environmental impacts, midpoint impact cate- gories were used in the LCA methodology. For this, the following midpoint categories of environmental impacts were used: climate

change (short term), freshwater acidification, freshwater ecotoxicity, freshwater eutrophication, human toxicity (cancer and non-cancer related), marine eutrophication, photochemical oxidant formation and terrestrial acidification.

A sensitivity analysis was conducted by varying the percentages of emissions during the storage stage to verify how an increase or decrease in emissions would affect the overall performance of each scenario, considering 15% and 25% variation in the emissions at this stage [85].

31: The LCA was applied to evaluate the environmental aspects and possible impacts associated with swine manure storage time and energy use by AD. The evaluation was performed according to the International Organization for Standardization (ISO14040 and 14,044) provided for any LCA study [37,38]. The software OpenLCA 1.10.2 [73] was used to perform the evaluation, (free open-source software), with detailed in- sights regarding the results and analysis performed, allowing for greater reliability and transparency.
The LCA approach was split into three scenarios to compare the

Swine raw manure Scenario 1||Anaerobic Digestion in Covered Lagoon (CLB) (Rec 60%)|Biogas|Electric energy potential|| Swine raw manure Scenario 2|Solid-Liquid Separation (screening and settling,30 min of HRT)|Liquid Fraction Solid Fraction|Anaerobic Digestion in CLB (Rec 95%) Anaerobic Digestion in CSTR (Rec 50%)|Biogas|Electric energy potential| Swine raw manure Scenario 3|Solid-Liquid Separation (screening and settling, 5 min of HRT)|Liquid Fraction Solid Fraction|Anaerobic Digestion in CLB (Rec 67%) Anaerobic Digestion in CSTR (Rec 50%)|Biogas|Electric energy potential|
Fig. 2. Hypothetical scenarios to estimate the energy potential with or without use of solid-liquid separation.

environmental profiles of swine manure treatment systems (Fig. 3). The of scenarios included manure production and storage in swine produc- tion installations; manure organic matter stabilization followed by field application and/or biogas production, or electricity and heat cogene- ration at the combined heat and power (CHP) plant; AD digestate stor- age and application as fertilizer.

The baseline was comprised of the storage of waste in the in- stallations (~8d) [74], followed by stabilization in an open storage tank (120 d) [75], transportation to the field, and soil application as a fer- tilizer source (N, P, and K). Scenario 1 included manure storage in the installation (8 d), followed by organic matter degradation by AD (30 d), and storage of digestate in an open storage tank (60 d). Then, the digestate was applied as fertilizer in the soil (after 60 d of storage, a hot spot for emissions in LCA studies of biogas systems), and the gaseous fraction (biogas) could be used for electricity and heat production. In Scenario 2, the swine manure storage in the installations was reduced to 3 d. The effluent was pretreated by before AD of both fractions. The biological process occurred in separate reactors because of to the con- centration of manure solids. As in Scenario 1, the liquid fraction was also used as a fertilizer and gaseous product for heat and electricity pro- duction (Fig. 3). For all scenarios, the impacts related to the construction steps of the physical installations were not included in the assessment, because they were not a result of changes in manure management, and the objective was to verify the impacts associated with effluent storage time and biogas production.

The LCA modeling for the current study was projected for the treatment of 1 m³ of swine manure. The life-cycle inventory (LCI) was constructed to understand the magnitude and significance of the po- tential environmental impacts of the effluents. LCI considered the pro- cess involved (and corresponding inputs and outputs) and the emissions and energetic demands from each phase. To understand and describe the consequences of a decision (Baseline, Scenario 1 or 2) for the environ- ment, we used some marginal data from the Ecoinvent 3.6 [76] data- base, data estimation based on the relevant literature, the inventory described by Cherubini et al. [77] and the experiments developed in present the study (Table More detailed information on the inventory estimates is provided in the Supplementary Material.

IMPACT World+, a globally regionalized life-cycle impact assess- ment method, was used to evaluate the described systems (Table 2). The method integrates Latin America and relates spatial variability to pri- ority elementary flows in a regionalized way [84]. Because the scenario in this study refers to environmental impacts, midpoint impact cate- gories were used in the LCA methodology. For this, the following midpoint categories of environmental impacts were used: climate

change (short term), freshwater acidification, freshwater ecotoxicity, freshwater eutrophication, human toxicity (cancer and non-cancer related), marine eutrophication, photochemical oxidant formation and terrestrial acidification.

A sensitivity analysis was conducted by varying the percentages of emissions during the storage stage to verify how an increase or decrease in emissions would affect the overall performance of each scenario, considering 15% and 25% variation in the emissions at this stage [85].
32: Differences between the treatments over time were assessed by an analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey's test for means comparisons, both with p 0.05 considered statistically significant.

To describes the behavior of sludge volume production through storage, screening, settling and chemical flocculation over time, a simple linear regression was performed. The coefficient of determination (R2) describes the explanation percentage of the adjusted model.

Statistical analyses (ANOVA, Tukey test, and linear regression) and the mathematical modeling required for the kinetic analysis of the Gompertz model were performed using the software R version 3.6.3.
33: <header>3.1. Effect of swine manure storage on solid-liquid separation </header>

According to the data obtained for physicochemical parameters during manure storage (Fig. 4), it was possible to verify that the con- centrations of TN, K, and TP exhibited smaller variations over time, unlike TAN and TOC, which presented increases of approximately 20% and 5%, respectively. Popovic and Jensen [86] also fund that TP con- centration in swine manure did not significantly influence the storage process, during 43 weeks at 25 °C.

However, TS was the parameter most affected by storage time, with an average reduction of 10% in TS after 20 d of storage. Provolo et al. [87] also verified a pronounced reduction in TS (approximately 15%) when the waste manure was stored for 28 d. Shin et al. [88] assessed the effects of storage and acidification of swine manure on biogas produc- tion, attaining a decrease in TS of approximately 20% after 40 d of storage, and approximately 1-3% of this reduction was related to CH4 emission.

Perazzolo et al. [89] studied the behavior of cattle slurry stored in different seasons of the year and showed that the temperature was a determining factor in the acceleration of the waste degradation process

because it favors the growth of microorganisms. According to Browne et al. [90], countries with tropical climates have effluent storage limited to environmental conditions because they can promote significant losses of nutrients and greenhouse gases, and consequently, compromise sub- sequent treatment efficiency.

According to Moset et al. [91], manure storage has a direct influence on organic matter biodegradability, and consequently, on gas emissions. In addition to the carbon emitted as methane, stored waste releases considerable amounts of nitrogen (as nitrous oxide or ammonia) into the atmosphere, which is subsequently deposited in aquatic environments, triggering eutrophication and acidification. Finally, the agronomic and energetic potential of the waste are reduced [7,8,22,92].

Manure storage severely compromised the screening, the first step of SLS. In the first 5 d, the efficiency of TS retained by screening was 27.9 + 19.6%. After this period, the efficiency decreased to 3.7 + 2.7%. This effect was also observed with 8 d of manure storage by Kunz et al. [74], where the efficiency was reduced by 30%.

Mechanical screening-based techniques generally have low effi- ciency for solids removal (approximately 30% for TS). This is because the sieves act mainly on the suspended particles, which have a diameter greater than the sieve mesh [31,93,94]. Based on this, the efficiency achieved in the first days was consistent with the average efficiency of the process and was also compatible with the results obtained by Marti et al. [95]. The authors achieved 27% efficiency in TS removal from

swine manure, whereas for dissolved fractions such as ammoniacal ni- trogen, they obtained an efficiency approximately 3.2%. In the present study, for the first 5 d, the efficiency of TAN removal was 6.7 + 2.6%.

For the separations conducted after screening, the manure age had a significant influence on the concentrations of some of the analyzed physicochemical parameters (Table 3). According to the Tukey test (p < 0.05), VS, TN, and TAN did not change significantly over time. In gen- eral, it was possible to verify an increase in TAN, TP, K, and TOC con- centrations in the supernatants of the separation techniques adopted.

On average, the TS removal efficiency after 19 d was 59.5 + 0.2% and 75.9 + 0.2% for settling and chemical flocculation, respectively. Regarding screening and settling using the SLS technique, the removal efficiencies of K were 7.3 + 0.3%, TP 61.2 + 0.2%, and TAN 15.1 + 0.3%; for screening and chemical flocculation, the efficiencies were 8.9 + 0.2%, 66.9 + 0.2% and 14.8 + 0.1%, respectively. Riaño and García- González [32] applied screening and chemical flocculation as SLS ap- proaches and obtained efficiencies similar to those of the present study, removing 68.3% of TS, 22% of TKN and 76.6% of TP.

According to the results of the SLS supernatant over time (Table 3), there was an increase in the concentration of the analyzed parameters, which was mostly because by a reduction in separation efficiency, and some transformations that occurred in the manure supported these in- crements. It is well known that for TP, the soluble fraction in swine waste is present in orthophosphate form, but most of it is associated with

Fig. 4. Swine raw manure physicochemical parameters concentration as a function of storage time. A) Relation between swine raw manure (C) and solids and TOC; B) Relation between swine raw manure (Co) and mineral forms. Co: TS:17.5 g L-1, FS: 4.6 g L-1; VS: 12.9 g L-1, TOC: 3.95 g L-1; TN: 1.83 g L-¹ K: 0.55 TP: 0.41 g L-1: TAN: 0.97 g L-¹.
TS, supporting the high efficiency of removal [94].

However, with solid decomposition, phosphorous tends to solubilize during manure storage and consequently its transformation into insol- uble species decreases over time [86,96], corroborating the increase observed in this work. The TOC increment observed in the liquid frac- tion from SLS was a consequence of manure degradation, which resulted in organic matter solubility, whereas part of it could be lost as methane [97].

The degradation of organic matter also leads to an increase in TAN concentration (Table 3) because of the ammonification process that occurs in waste with nitrogen, such as swine manure, caused by organic nitrogen degradation [98]. Hui et al. [99] stated that degradation caused by storage promotes an increase in such parameters in the liquid frac- tion. This reduction is directly reflected in subsequent treatment pro- cesses (i.e., AD, initrification/denitrification) and mainly in SLS efficiencies [30,90].

Comparing the two SLS techniques, chemical flocculation showed superior efficiency. In the settling process, mainly discrete particles will be removed, whereas chemical flocculation also removes suspended particles, substantiating the difference [100-102]. However, it is important to highlight that although the settling showed lower perfor- mance, the difference may not imply that flocculation should be adopted instead of settling once the cost associated with chemical flocculation makes the system onerous. It corresponds to approximately 30% of the operating cost of the treatment plant. This demonstrated the screening and settling approach as a considerable and applicable strategy for SLS of swine manure [33,66,103].

Additionally, the storage time also influenced the volume of sludge generated in different stages and with different separation techniques (Fig. 5a). As a consequence of reduced screening efficiency, the sludge volume retained in the sieve is also reduced by 52 + 0.5%, which in- terferes with the settleable solids volume (Fig. 5b), increasing in volume because of the degradation of the solid fraction [22,88]. The sludge from chemical flocculation tends to be more stable because of the use of chemical products, and does not show as much interference from the degradation process during sludge densification (Fig. 5c) [34,101].

The observed settleable solid volumes were consistent with those documented by Fragoso et al. [100], wherein the produced sludge vol- ume after 30 min of settling was 400 mL L-¹. The sludge volume ob- tained in the chemical flocculation tests (600 + 71 mL was close to that found by Steinmetz et al. [61], who applied the same concentration of PAM and TAN during the SLS.

The generation of sludge in treatment plants is an important issue, and because of the costs associated with its management, it deserves attention. Hence, there are several technologies that can be used in sludge treatment, ranging from agronomic use to energy reuse by AD [104-106]. Thus, it is evident that manure storage time should be reduced to avoid compromising the sludge volume generated, avoid the
emission of pollutant gases, and improve the entire management of swine manure treatment routes.

Manure storage severely compromised the screening, the first step of SLS. In the first 5 d, the efficiency of TS retained by screening was 27.9 + 19.6%. After this period, the efficiency decreased to 3.7 + 2.7%. This effect was also observed with 8 d of manure storage by Kunz et al. [74], where the efficiency was reduced by 30%.

Mechanical screening-based techniques generally have low effi- ciency for solids removal (approximately 30% for TS). This is because the sieves act mainly on the suspended particles, which have a diameter greater than the sieve mesh [31,93,94]. Based on this, the efficiency achieved in the first days was consistent with the average efficiency of the process and was also compatible with the results obtained by Marti et al. [95]. The authors achieved 27% efficiency in TS removal from

swine manure, whereas for dissolved fractions such as ammoniacal ni- trogen, they obtained an efficiency approximately 3.2%. In the present study, for the first 5 d, the efficiency of TAN removal was 6.7 + 2.6%.

For the separations conducted after screening, the manure age had a significant influence on the concentrations of some of the analyzed physicochemical parameters (Table 3). According to the Tukey test (p < 0.05), VS, TN, and TAN did not change significantly over time. In gen- eral, it was possible to verify an increase in TAN, TP, K, and TOC con- centrations in the supernatants of the separation techniques adopted.

On average, the TS removal efficiency after 19 d was 59.5 + 0.2% and 75.9 + 0.2% for settling and chemical flocculation, respectively. Regarding screening and settling using the SLS technique, the removal efficiencies of K were 7.3 + 0.3%, TP 61.2 + 0.2%, and TAN 15.1 + 0.3%; for screening and chemical flocculation, the efficiencies were 8.9 + 0.2%, 66.9 + 0.2% and 14.8 + 0.1%, respectively. Riaño and García- González [32] applied screening and chemical flocculation as SLS ap- proaches and obtained efficiencies similar to those of the present study, removing 68.3% of TS, 22% of TKN and 76.6% of TP.

According to the results of the SLS supernatant over time (Table 3), there was an increase in the concentration of the analyzed parameters, which was mostly because by a reduction in separation efficiency, and some transformations that occurred in the manure supported these in- crements. It is well known that for TP, the soluble fraction in swine waste is present in orthophosphate form, but most of it is associated with

Fig. 4. Swine raw manure physicochemical parameters concentration as a function of storage time. A) Relation between swine raw manure (C) and solids and TOC; B) Relation between swine raw manure (Co) and mineral forms. Co: TS:17.5 g L-1, FS: 4.6 g L-1; VS: 12.9 g L-1, TOC: 3.95 g L-1; TN: 1.83 g L-¹ K: 0.55 TP: 0.41 g L-1: TAN: 0.97 g L-¹.
TS, supporting the high efficiency of removal [94].

However, with solid decomposition, phosphorous tends to solubilize during manure storage and consequently its transformation into insol- uble species decreases over time [86,96], corroborating the increase observed in this work. The TOC increment observed in the liquid frac- tion from SLS was a consequence of manure degradation, which resulted in organic matter solubility, whereas part of it could be lost as methane [97].

The degradation of organic matter also leads to an increase in TAN concentration (Table 3) because of the ammonification process that occurs in waste with nitrogen, such as swine manure, caused by organic nitrogen degradation [98]. Hui et al. [99] stated that degradation caused by storage promotes an increase in such parameters in the liquid frac- tion. This reduction is directly reflected in subsequent treatment pro- cesses (i.e., AD, initrification/denitrification) and mainly in SLS efficiencies [30,90].

Comparing the two SLS techniques, chemical flocculation showed superior efficiency. In the settling process, mainly discrete particles will be removed, whereas chemical flocculation also removes suspended particles, substantiating the difference [100-102]. However, it is important to highlight that although the settling showed lower perfor- mance, the difference may not imply that flocculation should be adopted instead of settling once the cost associated with chemical flocculation makes the system onerous. It corresponds to approximately 30% of the operating cost of the treatment plant. This demonstrated the screening and settling approach as a considerable and applicable strategy for SLS of swine manure [33,66,103].

Additionally, the storage time also influenced the volume of sludge generated in different stages and with different separation techniques (Fig. 5a). As a consequence of reduced screening efficiency, the sludge volume retained in the sieve is also reduced by 52 + 0.5%, which in- terferes with the settleable solids volume (Fig. 5b), increasing in volume because of the degradation of the solid fraction [22,88]. The sludge from chemical flocculation tends to be more stable because of the use of chemical products, and does not show as much interference from the degradation process during sludge densification (Fig. 5c) [34,101].

The observed settleable solid volumes were consistent with those documented by Fragoso et al. [100], wherein the produced sludge vol- ume after 30 min of settling was 400 mL L-¹. The sludge volume ob- tained in the chemical flocculation tests (600 + 71 mL was close to that found by Steinmetz et al. [61], who applied the same concentration of PAM and TAN during the SLS.

The generation of sludge in treatment plants is an important issue, and because of the costs associated with its management, it deserves attention. Hence, there are several technologies that can be used in sludge treatment, ranging from agronomic use to energy reuse by AD [104-106]. Thus, it is evident that manure storage time should be reduced to avoid compromising the sludge volume generated, avoid the
emission of pollutant gases, and improve the entire management of swine manure treatment routes.
36: <header>3.1. Effect of swine manure storage on solid-liquid separation </header>

According to the data obtained for physicochemical parameters during manure storage (Fig. 4), it was possible to verify that the con- centrations of TN, K, and TP exhibited smaller variations over time, unlike TAN and TOC, which presented increases of approximately 20% and 5%, respectively. Popovic and Jensen [86] also fund that TP con- centration in swine manure did not significantly influence the storage process, during 43 weeks at 25 °C.

However, TS was the parameter most affected by storage time, with an average reduction of 10% in TS after 20 d of storage. Provolo et al. [87] also verified a pronounced reduction in TS (approximately 15%) when the waste manure was stored for 28 d. Shin et al. [88] assessed the effects of storage and acidification of swine manure on biogas produc- tion, attaining a decrease in TS of approximately 20% after 40 d of storage, and approximately 1-3% of this reduction was related to CH4 emission.

Perazzolo et al. [89] studied the behavior of cattle slurry stored in different seasons of the year and showed that the temperature was a determining factor in the acceleration of the waste degradation process

because it favors the growth of microorganisms. According to Browne et al. [90], countries with tropical climates have effluent storage limited to environmental conditions because they can promote significant losses of nutrients and greenhouse gases, and consequently, compromise sub- sequent treatment efficiency.

According to Moset et al. [91], manure storage has a direct influence on organic matter biodegradability, and consequently, on gas emissions. In addition to the carbon emitted as methane, stored waste releases considerable amounts of nitrogen (as nitrous oxide or ammonia) into the atmosphere, which is subsequently deposited in aquatic environments, triggering eutrophication and acidification. Finally, the agronomic and energetic potential of the waste are reduced [7,8,22,92].

Manure storage severely compromised the screening, the first step of SLS. In the first 5 d, the efficiency of TS retained by screening was 27.9 + 19.6%. After this period, the efficiency decreased to 3.7 + 2.7%. This effect was also observed with 8 d of manure storage by Kunz et al. [74], where the efficiency was reduced by 30%.

Mechanical screening-based techniques generally have low effi- ciency for solids removal (approximately 30% for TS). This is because the sieves act mainly on the suspended particles, which have a diameter greater than the sieve mesh [31,93,94]. Based on this, the efficiency achieved in the first days was consistent with the average efficiency of the process and was also compatible with the results obtained by Marti et al. [95]. The authors achieved 27% efficiency in TS removal from

swine manure, whereas for dissolved fractions such as ammoniacal ni- trogen, they obtained an efficiency approximately 3.2%. In the present study, for the first 5 d, the efficiency of TAN removal was 6.7 + 2.6%.

For the separations conducted after screening, the manure age had a significant influence on the concentrations of some of the analyzed physicochemical parameters (Table 3). According to the Tukey test (p < 0.05), VS, TN, and TAN did not change significantly over time. In gen- eral, it was possible to verify an increase in TAN, TP, K, and TOC con- centrations in the supernatants of the separation techniques adopted.

On average, the TS removal efficiency after 19 d was 59.5 + 0.2% and 75.9 + 0.2% for settling and chemical flocculation, respectively. Regarding screening and settling using the SLS technique, the removal efficiencies of K were 7.3 + 0.3%, TP 61.2 + 0.2%, and TAN 15.1 + 0.3%; for screening and chemical flocculation, the efficiencies were 8.9 + 0.2%, 66.9 + 0.2% and 14.8 + 0.1%, respectively. Riaño and García- González [32] applied screening and chemical flocculation as SLS ap- proaches and obtained efficiencies similar to those of the present study, removing 68.3% of TS, 22% of TKN and 76.6% of TP.

According to the results of the SLS supernatant over time (Table 3), there was an increase in the concentration of the analyzed parameters, which was mostly because by a reduction in separation efficiency, and some transformations that occurred in the manure supported these in- crements. It is well known that for TP, the soluble fraction in swine waste is present in orthophosphate form, but most of it is associated with

Fig. 4. Swine raw manure physicochemical parameters concentration as a function of storage time. A) Relation between swine raw manure (C) and solids and TOC; B) Relation between swine raw manure (Co) and mineral forms. Co: TS:17.5 g L-1, FS: 4.6 g L-1; VS: 12.9 g L-1, TOC: 3.95 g L-1; TN: 1.83 g L-¹ K: 0.55 TP: 0.41 g L-1: TAN: 0.97 g L-¹.
TS, supporting the high efficiency of removal [94].

However, with solid decomposition, phosphorous tends to solubilize during manure storage and consequently its transformation into insol- uble species decreases over time [86,96], corroborating the increase observed in this work. The TOC increment observed in the liquid frac- tion from SLS was a consequence of manure degradation, which resulted in organic matter solubility, whereas part of it could be lost as methane [97].

The degradation of organic matter also leads to an increase in TAN concentration (Table 3) because of the ammonification process that occurs in waste with nitrogen, such as swine manure, caused by organic nitrogen degradation [98]. Hui et al. [99] stated that degradation caused by storage promotes an increase in such parameters in the liquid frac- tion. This reduction is directly reflected in subsequent treatment pro- cesses (i.e., AD, initrification/denitrification) and mainly in SLS efficiencies [30,90].

Comparing the two SLS techniques, chemical flocculation showed superior efficiency. In the settling process, mainly discrete particles will be removed, whereas chemical flocculation also removes suspended particles, substantiating the difference [100-102]. However, it is important to highlight that although the settling showed lower perfor- mance, the difference may not imply that flocculation should be adopted instead of settling once the cost associated with chemical flocculation makes the system onerous. It corresponds to approximately 30% of the operating cost of the treatment plant. This demonstrated the screening and settling approach as a considerable and applicable strategy for SLS of swine manure [33,66,103].

Additionally, the storage time also influenced the volume of sludge generated in different stages and with different separation techniques (Fig. 5a). As a consequence of reduced screening efficiency, the sludge volume retained in the sieve is also reduced by 52 + 0.5%, which in- terferes with the settleable solids volume (Fig. 5b), increasing in volume because of the degradation of the solid fraction [22,88]. The sludge from chemical flocculation tends to be more stable because of the use of chemical products, and does not show as much interference from the degradation process during sludge densification (Fig. 5c) [34,101].

The observed settleable solid volumes were consistent with those documented by Fragoso et al. [100], wherein the produced sludge vol- ume after 30 min of settling was 400 mL L-¹. The sludge volume ob- tained in the chemical flocculation tests (600 + 71 mL was close to that found by Steinmetz et al. [61], who applied the same concentration of PAM and TAN during the SLS.

The generation of sludge in treatment plants is an important issue, and because of the costs associated with its management, it deserves attention. Hence, there are several technologies that can be used in sludge treatment, ranging from agronomic use to energy reuse by AD [104-106]. Thus, it is evident that manure storage time should be reduced to avoid compromising the sludge volume generated, avoid the
emission of pollutant gases, and improve the entire management of swine manure treatment routes.
39: <header>3.1. Effect of swine manure storage on solid-liquid separation </header>

According to the data obtained for physicochemical parameters during manure storage (Fig. 4), it was possible to verify that the con- centrations of TN, K, and TP exhibited smaller variations over time, unlike TAN and TOC, which presented increases of approximately 20% and 5%, respectively. Popovic and Jensen [86] also fund that TP con- centration in swine manure did not significantly influence the storage process, during 43 weeks at 25 °C.

However, TS was the parameter most affected by storage time, with an average reduction of 10% in TS after 20 d of storage. Provolo et al. [87] also verified a pronounced reduction in TS (approximately 15%) when the waste manure was stored for 28 d. Shin et al. [88] assessed the effects of storage and acidification of swine manure on biogas produc- tion, attaining a decrease in TS of approximately 20% after 40 d of storage, and approximately 1-3% of this reduction was related to CH4 emission.

Perazzolo et al. [89] studied the behavior of cattle slurry stored in different seasons of the year and showed that the temperature was a determining factor in the acceleration of the waste degradation process

[88] assessed the effects of storage and acidification of swine manure on biogas produc- tion, attaining a decrease in TS of approximately 20% after 40 d of storage, and approximately 1-3% of this reduction was related to CH4 emission.

Perazzolo et al. [89] studied the behavior of cattle slurry stored in different seasons of the year and showed that the temperature was a determining factor in the acceleration of the waste degradation process

because it favors the growth of microorganisms. According to Browne et al. [90], countries with tropical climates have effluent storage limited to environmental conditions because they can promote significant losses of nutrients and greenhouse gases, and consequently, compromise sub- sequent treatment efficiency.

According to Moset et al. [91], manure storage has a direct influence on organic matter biodegradability, and consequently, on gas emissions. In addition to the carbon emitted as methane, stored waste releases considerable amounts of nitrogen (as nitrous oxide or ammonia) into the atmosphere, which is subsequently deposited in aquatic environments, triggering eutrophication and acidification. Finally, the agronomic and energetic potential of the waste are reduced [7,8,22,92].

Manure storage severely compromised the screening, the first step of SLS. In the first 5 d, the efficiency of TS retained by screening was 27.9 + 19.6%. After this period, the efficiency decreased to 3.7 + 2.7%. This effect was also observed with 8 d of manure storage by Kunz et al. [74], where the efficiency was reduced by 30%.

Mechanical screening-based techniques generally have low effi- ciency for solids removal (approximately 30% for TS). This is because the sieves act mainly on the suspended particles, which have a diameter greater than the sieve mesh [31,93,94]. Based on this, the efficiency achieved in the first days was consistent with the average efficiency of the process and was also compatible with the results obtained by Marti et al. [95]. The authors achieved 27% efficiency in TS removal from

swine manure, whereas for dissolved fractions such as ammoniacal ni- trogen, they obtained an efficiency approximately 3.2%. In the present study, for the first 5 d, the efficiency of TAN removal was 6.7 + 2.6%.

For the separations conducted after screening, the manure age had a significant influence on the concentrations of some of the analyzed physicochemical parameters (Table 3). According to the Tukey test (p < 0.05), VS, TN, and TAN did not change significantly over time. In gen- eral, it was possible to verify an increase in TAN, TP, K, and TOC con- centrations in the supernatants of the separation techniques adopted.

On average, the TS removal efficiency after 19 d was 59.5 + 0.2% and 75.9 + 0.2% for settling and chemical flocculation, respectively. Regarding screening and settling using the SLS technique, the removal efficiencies of K were 7.3 + 0.3%, TP 61.2 + 0.2%, and TAN 15.1 + 0.3%; for screening and chemical flocculation, the efficiencies were 8.9 + 0.2%, 66.9 + 0.2% and 14.8 + 0.1%, respectively. Riaño and García- González [32] applied screening and chemical flocculation as SLS ap- proaches and obtained efficiencies similar to those of the present study, removing 68.3% of TS, 22% of TKN and 76.6% of TP.

According to the results of the SLS supernatant over time (Table 3), there was an increase in the concentration of the analyzed parameters, which was mostly because by a reduction in separation efficiency, and some transformations that occurred in the manure supported these in- crements. It is well known that for TP, the soluble fraction in swine waste is present in orthophosphate form, but most of it is associated with

Fig. 4. Swine raw manure physicochemical parameters concentration as a function of storage time. A) Relation between swine raw manure (C) and solids and TOC; B) Relation between swine raw manure (Co) and mineral forms. Co: TS:17.5 g L-1, FS: 4.6 g L-1; VS: 12.9 g L-1, TOC: 3.95 g L-1; TN: 1.83 g L-¹ K: 0.55 TP: 0.41 g L-1: TAN: 0.97 g L-¹.
TS, supporting the high efficiency of removal [94].

However, with solid decomposition, phosphorous tends to solubilize during manure storage and consequently its transformation into insol- uble species decreases over time [86,96], corroborating the increase observed in this work. The TOC increment observed in the liquid frac- tion from SLS was a consequence of manure degradation, which resulted in organic matter solubility, whereas part of it could be lost as methane [97].

The degradation of organic matter also leads to an increase in TAN concentration (Table 3) because of the ammonification process that occurs in waste with nitrogen, such as swine manure, caused by organic nitrogen degradation [98]. Hui et al. [99] stated that degradation caused by storage promotes an increase in such parameters in the liquid frac- tion. This reduction is directly reflected in subsequent treatment pro- cesses (i.e., AD, initrification/denitrification) and mainly in SLS efficiencies [30,90].

Comparing the two SLS techniques, chemical flocculation showed superior efficiency. In the settling process, mainly discrete particles will be removed, whereas chemical flocculation also removes suspended particles, substantiating the difference [100-102]. However, it is important to highlight that although the settling showed lower perfor- mance, the difference may not imply that flocculation should be adopted instead of settling once the cost associated with chemical flocculation makes the system onerous. It corresponds to approximately 30% of the operating cost of the treatment plant. This demonstrated the screening and settling approach as a considerable and applicable strategy for SLS of swine manure [33,66,103].

Additionally, the storage time also influenced the volume of sludge generated in different stages and with different separation techniques (Fig. 5a). As a consequence of reduced screening efficiency, the sludge volume retained in the sieve is also reduced by 52 + 0.5%, which in- terferes with the settleable solids volume (Fig. 5b), increasing in volume because of the degradation of the solid fraction [22,88]. The sludge from chemical flocculation tends to be more stable because of the use of chemical products, and does not show as much interference from the degradation process during sludge densification (Fig. 5c) [34,101].

The observed settleable solid volumes were consistent with those documented by Fragoso et al. [100], wherein the produced sludge vol- ume after 30 min of settling was 400 mL L-¹. The sludge volume ob- tained in the chemical flocculation tests (600 + 71 mL was close to that found by Steinmetz et al. [61], who applied the same concentration of PAM and TAN during the SLS.

The generation of sludge in treatment plants is an important issue, and because of the costs associated with its management, it deserves attention. Hence, there are several technologies that can be used in sludge treatment, ranging from agronomic use to energy reuse by AD [104-106]. Thus, it is evident that manure storage time should be reduced to avoid compromising the sludge volume generated, avoid the
emission of pollutant gases, and improve the entire management of swine manure treatment routes.

According to the data obtained for physicochemical parameters during manure storage (Fig. 4), it was possible to verify that the con- centrations of TN, K, and TP exhibited smaller variations over time, unlike TAN and TOC, which presented increases of approximately 20% and 5%, respectively. Popovic and Jensen [86] also fund that TP con- centration in swine manure did not significantly influence the storage process, during 43 weeks at 25 °C.

However, TS was the parameter most affected by storage time, with an average reduction of 10% in TS after 20 d of storage. Provolo et al. [87] also verified a pronounced reduction in TS (approximately 15%) when the waste manure was stored for 28 d. Shin et al. [88] assessed the effects of storage and acidification of swine manure on biogas produc- tion, attaining a decrease in TS of approximately 20% after 40 d of storage, and approximately 1-3% of this reduction was related to CH4 emission.

Perazzolo et al. [89] studied the behavior of cattle slurry stored in different seasons of the year and showed that the temperature was a determining factor in the acceleration of the waste degradation process

emission of pollutant gases, and improve the entire management of swine manure treatment routes.
44: <header>3.1. Effect of swine manure storage on solid-liquid separation </header>

According to the data obtained for physicochemical parameters during manure storage (Fig. 4), it was possible to verify that the con- centrations of TN, K, and TP exhibited smaller variations over time, unlike TAN and TOC, which presented increases of approximately 20% and 5%, respectively. Popovic and Jensen [86] also fund that TP con- centration in swine manure did not significantly influence the storage process, during 43 weeks at 25 °C.

However, TS was the parameter most affected by storage time, with an average reduction of 10% in TS after 20 d of storage. Provolo et al. [87] also verified a pronounced reduction in TS (approximately 15%) when the waste manure was stored for 28 d. Shin et al. [88] assessed the effects of storage and acidification of swine manure on biogas produc- tion, attaining a decrease in TS of approximately 20% after 40 d of storage, and approximately 1-3% of this reduction was related to CH4 emission.

Perazzolo et al. [89] studied the behavior of cattle slurry stored in different seasons of the year and showed that the temperature was a determining factor in the acceleration of the waste degradation process
