because it favors the growth of microorganisms. According to Browne et al. [90], countries with tropical climates have effluent storage limited to environmental conditions because they can promote significant losses of nutrients and greenhouse gases, and consequently, compromise sub- sequent treatment efficiency.

According to Moset et al. [91], manure storage has a direct influence on organic matter biodegradability, and consequently, on gas emissions. In addition to the carbon emitted as methane, stored waste releases considerable amounts of nitrogen (as nitrous oxide or ammonia) into the atmosphere, which is subsequently deposited in aquatic environments, triggering eutrophication and acidification. Finally, the agronomic and energetic potential of the waste are reduced [7,8,22,92].

Manure storage severely compromised the screening, the first step of SLS. In the first 5 d, the efficiency of TS retained by screening was 27.9 + 19.6%. After this period, the efficiency decreased to 3.7 + 2.7%. This effect was also observed with 8 d of manure storage by Kunz et al. [74], where the efficiency was reduced by 30%.

Mechanical screening-based techniques generally have low effi- ciency for solids removal (approximately 30% for TS). This is because the sieves act mainly on the suspended particles, which have a diameter greater than the sieve mesh [31,93,94]. Based on this, the efficiency achieved in the first days was consistent with the average efficiency of the process and was also compatible with the results obtained by Marti et al. [95]. The authors achieved 27% efficiency in TS removal from

swine manure, whereas for dissolved fractions such as ammoniacal ni- trogen, they obtained an efficiency approximately 3.2%. In the present study, for the first 5 d, the efficiency of TAN removal was 6.7 + 2.6%.

For the separations conducted after screening, the manure age had a significant influence on the concentrations of some of the analyzed physicochemical parameters (Table 3). According to the Tukey test (p < 0.05), VS, TN, and TAN did not change significantly over time. In gen- eral, it was possible to verify an increase in TAN, TP, K, and TOC con- centrations in the supernatants of the separation techniques adopted.

On average, the TS removal efficiency after 19 d was 59.5 + 0.2% and 75.9 + 0.2% for settling and chemical flocculation, respectively. Regarding screening and settling using the SLS technique, the removal efficiencies of K were 7.3 + 0.3%, TP 61.2 + 0.2%, and TAN 15.1 + 0.3%; for screening and chemical flocculation, the efficiencies were 8.9 + 0.2%, 66.9 + 0.2% and 14.8 + 0.1%, respectively. Riaño and García- González [32] applied screening and chemical flocculation as SLS ap- proaches and obtained efficiencies similar to those of the present study, removing 68.3% of TS, 22% of TKN and 76.6% of TP.

According to the results of the SLS supernatant over time (Table 3), there was an increase in the concentration of the analyzed parameters, which was mostly because by a reduction in separation efficiency, and some transformations that occurred in the manure supported these in- crements. It is well known that for TP, the soluble fraction in swine waste is present in orthophosphate form, but most of it is associated with

Fig. 4. Swine raw manure physicochemical parameters concentration as a function of storage time. A) Relation between swine raw manure (C) and solids and TOC; B) Relation between swine raw manure (Co) and mineral forms. Co: TS:17.5 g L-1, FS: 4.6 g L-1; VS: 12.9 g L-1, TOC: 3.95 g L-1; TN: 1.83 g L-¹ K: 0.55 TP: 0.41 g L-1: TAN: 0.97 g L-¹.
TS, supporting the high efficiency of removal [94].

However, with solid decomposition, phosphorous tends to solubilize during manure storage and consequently its transformation into insol- uble species decreases over time [86,96], corroborating the increase observed in this work. The TOC increment observed in the liquid frac- tion from SLS was a consequence of manure degradation, which resulted in organic matter solubility, whereas part of it could be lost as methane [97].

The degradation of organic matter also leads to an increase in TAN concentration (Table 3) because of the ammonification process that occurs in waste with nitrogen, such as swine manure, caused by organic nitrogen degradation [98]. Hui et al. [99] stated that degradation caused by storage promotes an increase in such parameters in the liquid frac- tion. This reduction is directly reflected in subsequent treatment pro- cesses (i.e., AD, initrification/denitrification) and mainly in SLS efficiencies [30,90].

Comparing the two SLS techniques, chemical flocculation showed superior efficiency. In the settling process, mainly discrete particles will be removed, whereas chemical flocculation also removes suspended particles, substantiating the difference [100-102]. However, it is important to highlight that although the settling showed lower perfor- mance, the difference may not imply that flocculation should be adopted instead of settling once the cost associated with chemical flocculation makes the system onerous. It corresponds to approximately 30% of the operating cost of the treatment plant. This demonstrated the screening and settling approach as a considerable and applicable strategy for SLS of swine manure [33,66,103].

Additionally, the storage time also influenced the volume of sludge generated in different stages and with different separation techniques (Fig. 5a). As a consequence of reduced screening efficiency, the sludge volume retained in the sieve is also reduced by 52 + 0.5%, which in- terferes with the settleable solids volume (Fig. 5b), increasing in volume because of the degradation of the solid fraction [22,88]. The sludge from chemical flocculation tends to be more stable because of the use of chemical products, and does not show as much interference from the degradation process during sludge densification (Fig. 5c) [34,101].

The observed settleable solid volumes were consistent with those documented by Fragoso et al. [100], wherein the produced sludge vol- ume after 30 min of settling was 400 mL L-¹. The sludge volume ob- tained in the chemical flocculation tests (600 + 71 mL was close to that found by Steinmetz et al. [61], who applied the same concentration of PAM and TAN during the SLS.

The generation of sludge in treatment plants is an important issue, and because of the costs associated with its management, it deserves attention. Hence, there are several technologies that can be used in sludge treatment, ranging from agronomic use to energy reuse by AD [104-106]. Thus, it is evident that manure storage time should be reduced to avoid compromising the sludge volume generated, avoid the
emission of pollutant gases, and improve the entire management of swine manure treatment routes.
45: <header>3.2. Effect of solid-liquid separation of swine manure on BMP </header>

As concluded, the manure storage time should not be longer than 5 d therefore, the waste used was collected with a maximum of 3 d of storage. Several studies have mentioned the effects of storage on organic matter degradation and the consequent adverse outcomes regarding biogas production. Not only in the pre-treatment stage, but also in the AD stage, storage negatively affects the treatment system.

Shin et al. [88] investigated the effects of storage on GHG emissions and biogas production and noted that waste stored for 40 d emits approximately 3.7 kgCO2 eq. and reduced the effluent BMP by up to 40%. Im et al. [8] also studied the storage effect during 80 d at 35 °C on cattle manure degradation and stated that it emitted 375.1 kgCO2 eq. Tonvs and the biogas potential was reduced by 42%. It is evident that stockpiling affects the treatment system not only in terms of process efficiency, but also in terms of the environmental damage it can cause because of the emissions associated with this practice, compro- mising environmental quality.

To better understand the influence of SLS on biogas production, the mass balances of the fractions under study were initially determined (Table 4). It was possible to verify that screening generates a sludge volume of approximately 3.5 + 2.0% and settling of approximately 10.0 + 5.0%, both related to the input volume into the SLS system. Yang et al. [27] obtained similar proportions in relation to the SLS volumetric balance, with 85% of the final volume related to the supernatant and 15% to the sludge from the separation process, using a separation system composed of a hydraulic wedge-shaped sieve followed by 3 h of settling. Yang et al. [33] obtained volumetric efficiencies ranging from 10 to 30% in sludge generation in the process of SLS of swine manure through a settling column made of plexiglass.

The composition of animal manure is influenced by several factors, including animal diet, production phase, age, water consumption, and management system, which directly affect the characteristics of the final material. Consequently, a solid fraction is present, which supports the divergent values in the literature and the data observed in this study on the removal during the separation stages, both by sieving and sedi- mentation, in addition to operational differences in the SSLstage itself (e. g., mesh of sieve, sedimentation time, use of centrifuges) [32,33].

Even with the HRT changes imposed on SLS, the separation ratios were maintained, as shown in Table 4, based on the volumetric balance. However, the separation efficiency varied depending on the HRT employed for each SLS test. As the settling time increased, the concen- trations of the analyzed parameters showed a tendency to decay in the supernatants, being concentrated in the sludge, as previously discussed. TS removal efficiencies obtained by SLS were higher than those report
Table 3 Swine manure characteristics (liquid fraction) after the solid-liquid separation process.

Showed no significant variation over time according to Tukey test (p 0.05). Note: Different lowercase letters indicate different means between days for each parameter inside the treatments and different uppercase letters indicate different means between treatments for each day and parameter, according to Tukey test, p < 0.05.

by Zhang et al. [34], which achieved 40% after 120 min of SLS of swine wastes by settling. This difference can be explained by the storage time because the authors stocked the manure for 2 months, compromising the SLS.

After the SLS balances were determined, BMP tests were performed to compare the effect of SLS on AD. As presented by Jingura and Kamusoko [107], BMP tests are the basis for determining the perfor- mance of the AD process, being a high reliability and widely used method. These tests resulted in a higher methane yield value in the su- pernatant from settling with a 30 min HRT, as shown in Table 5. As the HRT in the settling step increased, the potential also increased, even reducing the solids concentration. There was an increase in the methane production potential up to 1.2 times compared to the raw manure value. Supernatants, in general, correspond to 30-90% of the swine manure methane potential.

Amaral et al. [23] also showed that BMP was higher in the super- natant from SLS. According to the authors, the organic matter of these fractions has greater bioavailable for AD, reducing the HRT of bio- digesters. This makes it possible to treat these fractions in high-loading rate reactors (i.e., upflow anaerobic sludge blanket, UASB) [17,71]. Different sedimentation times affect the composition of the fractions present in the substrate, not only in terms of VS, but also in relation to their biodegradability, resulting changes in the C/N ratio and potentially inhibiting the process by the concentration of inhibitors in a particular fraction. Based on this, it was possible to verify the optimal operation conditions for the pre-treatment stage, considering not only the effi- ciency of the separation process but also the performance of the AD stage.

The sludge retained in the sieve exhibited the highest methane production, 51.38 + 3.11m³ N CH4 fraction, with a BMP similar to that of other analyzed fractions. In a general way, all sludge fractions had a higher BMP because of their high solids concentration (1-12%) compared to that of the supernatants (0.3-0.8%). Furthermore, the sludge corresponds to 10-60% of the biogas potential of swine manure, but with an increase of up to 14% in methane production in relation to that of raw manure.

Rico et al. [26] studied the effects of chemical treatment and screening at the technique for the separation of dairy cattle manure and found changes in the BMPs of the different fractions obtained in the SLS. The dairy manure changed from 0.320 m³ kgvs to 0.580 m³ kgvs in the separated liquid fraction and 0.258 m³ kgvs in the solid fraction. In addition to the increase in BMP, another important observation made by these authors was changes in the degradation times of the fractions. Although the liquid fraction took 21 d to reach 90% recovery of biogas, the solid fraction and raw manure took 48 and 52 d, respectively, to reach this percentage of recovery. This reinforces the discussion of the beneficial effect of SLS on the AD of animal waste, resulting in higher rates of biogas and methane production, giving higher loading capacity in digestion reactors with shorter retention times [30].

According to the results obtained in this study, the sludge fractions have the capacity to produce from 4.5 to 51.4 m³ of methane for each m³ of digested sludge. These values are approximately 7.5 times higher than that of the supernatants, which shows the capacity to produce from 1.58 to 3.45 of CH4 for each m³ of the digested fraction. Deng et al. [108] also reported that the biogas potential from sludge was approximately 6.7 times higher than that from SLS. Yang et al. [33] stated that because of the higher levels of solids (sludge fractions), a higher HRT may be needed, which influences reactor size. However, the methane yield was still higher than that of the digestion of raw manure.

Fig. 6 shows the relative behavior of the accumulated biogas yield in the stratified fractions compared to that of raw manure (Figure A1). The supernatants exhibited a production delay when compared to the sludge fractions. However, after 1.7 d the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) had an equal yield to that of raw manure. After the initial delay presented by the supernatants (compared to the other fractions) yield rates increased, and the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) stood out in relation to other fractions, with a yield 14% higher than that of the raw manure.

According to Cao et al. [109], this behavior occurs because the methane production rate is lower than that the hydrolysis at the beginning of the process. With an improvement in biodegradability caused by SLS, the hydrolysis and acidification rates are higher and methanogenesis is reversibly inhibited by acid accumulation. Because it

[88] investigated the effects of storage on GHG emissions and biogas production and noted that waste stored for 40 d emits approximately 3.7 kgCO2 eq. and reduced the effluent BMP by up to 40%. Im et al. [8] also studied the storage effect during 80 d at 35 °C on cattle manure degradation and stated that it emitted 375.1 kgCO2 eq. Tonvs and the biogas potential was reduced by 42%. It is evident that stockpiling affects the treatment system not only in terms of process efficiency, but also in terms of the environmental damage it can cause because of the emissions associated with this practice, compro- mising environmental quality.

To better understand the influence of SLS on biogas production, the mass balances of the fractions under study were initially determined (Table 4). It was possible to verify that screening generates a sludge volume of approximately 3.5 + 2.0% and settling of approximately 10.0 + 5.0%, both related to the input volume into the SLS system. Yang et al. [27] obtained similar proportions in relation to the SLS volumetric balance, with 85% of the final volume related to the supernatant and 15% to the sludge from the separation process, using a separation system composed of a hydraulic wedge-shaped sieve followed by 3 h of settling. Yang et al. [33] obtained volumetric efficiencies ranging from 10 to 30% in sludge generation in the process of SLS of swine manure through a settling column made of plexiglass.

The composition of animal manure is influenced by several factors, including animal diet, production phase, age, water consumption, and management system, which directly affect the characteristics of the final material. Consequently, a solid fraction is present, which supports the divergent values in the literature and the data observed in this study on the removal during the separation stages, both by sieving and sedi- mentation, in addition to operational differences in the SSLstage itself (e. g., mesh of sieve, sedimentation time, use of centrifuges) [32,33].

Even with the HRT changes imposed on SLS, the separation ratios were maintained, as shown in Table 4, based on the volumetric balance. However, the separation efficiency varied depending on the HRT employed for each SLS test. As the settling time increased, the concen- trations of the analyzed parameters showed a tendency to decay in the supernatants, being concentrated in the sludge, as previously discussed. TS removal efficiencies obtained by SLS were higher than those report
Table 3 Swine manure characteristics (liquid fraction) after the solid-liquid separation process.

Showed no significant variation over time according to Tukey test (p 0.05). Note: Different lowercase letters indicate different means between days for each parameter inside the treatments and different uppercase letters indicate different means between treatments for each day and parameter, according to Tukey test, p < 0.05.

by Zhang et al. [34], which achieved 40% after 120 min of SLS of swine wastes by settling. This difference can be explained by the storage time because the authors stocked the manure for 2 months, compromising the SLS.

After the SLS balances were determined, BMP tests were performed to compare the effect of SLS on AD. As presented by Jingura and Kamusoko [107], BMP tests are the basis for determining the perfor- mance of the AD process, being a high reliability and widely used method. These tests resulted in a higher methane yield value in the su- pernatant from settling with a 30 min HRT, as shown in Table 5. As the HRT in the settling step increased, the potential also increased, even reducing the solids concentration. There was an increase in the methane production potential up to 1.2 times compared to the raw manure value. Supernatants, in general, correspond to 30-90% of the swine manure methane potential.

Amaral et al. [23] also showed that BMP was higher in the super- natant from SLS. According to the authors, the organic matter of these fractions has greater bioavailable for AD, reducing the HRT of bio- digesters. This makes it possible to treat these fractions in high-loading rate reactors (i.e., upflow anaerobic sludge blanket, UASB) [17,71]. Different sedimentation times affect the composition of the fractions present in the substrate, not only in terms of VS, but also in relation to their biodegradability, resulting changes in the C/N ratio and potentially inhibiting the process by the concentration of inhibitors in a particular fraction. Based on this, it was possible to verify the optimal operation conditions for the pre-treatment stage, considering not only the effi- ciency of the separation process but also the performance of the AD stage.

The sludge retained in the sieve exhibited the highest methane production, 51.38 + 3.11m³ N CH4 fraction, with a BMP similar to that of other analyzed fractions. In a general way, all sludge fractions had a higher BMP because of their high solids concentration (1-12%) compared to that of the supernatants (0.3-0.8%). Furthermore, the sludge corresponds to 10-60% of the biogas potential of swine manure, but with an increase of up to 14% in methane production in relation to that of raw manure.

Rico et al. [26] studied the effects of chemical treatment and screening at the technique for the separation of dairy cattle manure and found changes in the BMPs of the different fractions obtained in the SLS. The dairy manure changed from 0.320 m³ kgvs to 0.580 m³ kgvs in the separated liquid fraction and 0.258 m³ kgvs in the solid fraction. In addition to the increase in BMP, another important observation made by these authors was changes in the degradation times of the fractions. Although the liquid fraction took 21 d to reach 90% recovery of biogas, the solid fraction and raw manure took 48 and 52 d, respectively, to reach this percentage of recovery. This reinforces the discussion of the beneficial effect of SLS on the AD of animal waste, resulting in higher rates of biogas and methane production, giving higher loading capacity in digestion reactors with shorter retention times [30].

According to the results obtained in this study, the sludge fractions have the capacity to produce from 4.5 to 51.4 m³ of methane for each m³ of digested sludge. These values are approximately 7.5 times higher than that of the supernatants, which shows the capacity to produce from 1.58 to 3.45 of CH4 for each m³ of the digested fraction. Deng et al. [108] also reported that the biogas potential from sludge was approximately 6.7 times higher than that from SLS. Yang et al. [33] stated that because of the higher levels of solids (sludge fractions), a higher HRT may be needed, which influences reactor size. However, the methane yield was still higher than that of the digestion of raw manure.

Fig. 6 shows the relative behavior of the accumulated biogas yield in the stratified fractions compared to that of raw manure (Figure A1). The supernatants exhibited a production delay when compared to the sludge fractions. However, after 1.7 d the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) had an equal yield to that of raw manure. After the initial delay presented by the supernatants (compared to the other fractions) yield rates increased, and the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) stood out in relation to other fractions, with a yield 14% higher than that of the raw manure.

According to Cao et al. [109], this behavior occurs because the methane production rate is lower than that the hydrolysis at the beginning of the process. With an improvement in biodegradability caused by SLS, the hydrolysis and acidification rates are higher and methanogenesis is reversibly inhibited by acid accumulation. Because it

is a reversible inhibition with biogas production, yield increases again (Fig. 6), as also reported by Jiang et al. [110]. resulting from SLS of swine manure, whereas for the solid fraction and the raw manure, the peak production ranged between 3 and 9 d. Even with the HRT changes imposed on SLS, the separation ratios were maintained, as shown in Table 4, based on the volumetric balance. However, the separation efficiency varied depending on the HRT employed for each SLS test. As the settling time increased, the concen- trations of the analyzed parameters showed a tendency to decay in the supernatants, being concentrated in the sludge, as previously discussed. TS removal efficiencies obtained by SLS were higher than those report
Table 3 Swine manure characteristics (liquid fraction) after the solid-liquid separation process.

Showed no significant variation over time according to Tukey test (p 0.05). Note: Different lowercase letters indicate different means between days for each parameter inside the treatments and different uppercase letters indicate different means between treatments for each day and parameter, according to Tukey test, p < 0.05.

by Zhang et al. [34], which achieved 40% after 120 min of SLS of swine wastes by settling. This difference can be explained by the storage time because the authors stocked the manure for 2 months, compromising the SLS.

After the SLS balances were determined, BMP tests were performed to compare the effect of SLS on AD. As presented by Jingura and Kamusoko [107], BMP tests are the basis for determining the perfor- mance of the AD process, being a high reliability and widely used method. These tests resulted in a higher methane yield value in the su- pernatant from settling with a 30 min HRT, as shown in Table 5. As the HRT in the settling step increased, the potential also increased, even reducing the solids concentration. There was an increase in the methane production potential up to 1.2 times compared to the raw manure value. Supernatants, in general, correspond to 30-90% of the swine manure methane potential.

Amaral et al. [23] also showed that BMP was higher in the super- natant from SLS. According to the authors, the organic matter of these fractions has greater bioavailable for AD, reducing the HRT of bio- digesters. This makes it possible to treat these fractions in high-loading rate reactors (i.e., upflow anaerobic sludge blanket, UASB) [17,71]. Different sedimentation times affect the composition of the fractions present in the substrate, not only in terms of VS, but also in relation to their biodegradability, resulting changes in the C/N ratio and potentially inhibiting the process by the concentration of inhibitors in a particular fraction. Based on this, it was possible to verify the optimal operation conditions for the pre-treatment stage, considering not only the effi- ciency of the separation process but also the performance of the AD stage.

The sludge retained in the sieve exhibited the highest methane production, 51.38 + 3.11m³ N CH4 fraction, with a BMP similar to that of other analyzed fractions. In a general way, all sludge fractions had a higher BMP because of their high solids concentration (1-12%) compared to that of the supernatants (0.3-0.8%). Furthermore, the sludge corresponds to 10-60% of the biogas potential of swine manure, but with an increase of up to 14% in methane production in relation to that of raw manure.

Rico et al. [26] studied the effects of chemical treatment and screening at the technique for the separation of dairy cattle manure and found changes in the BMPs of the different fractions obtained in the SLS. The dairy manure changed from 0.320 m³ kgvs to 0.580 m³ kgvs in the separated liquid fraction and 0.258 m³ kgvs in the solid fraction. In addition to the increase in BMP, another important observation made by these authors was changes in the degradation times of the fractions. Although the liquid fraction took 21 d to reach 90% recovery of biogas, the solid fraction and raw manure took 48 and 52 d, respectively, to reach this percentage of recovery. This reinforces the discussion of the beneficial effect of SLS on the AD of animal waste, resulting in higher rates of biogas and methane production, giving higher loading capacity in digestion reactors with shorter retention times [30].

According to the results obtained in this study, the sludge fractions have the capacity to produce from 4.5 to 51.4 m³ of methane for each m³ of digested sludge. These values are approximately 7.5 times higher than that of the supernatants, which shows the capacity to produce from 1.58 to 3.45 of CH4 for each m³ of the digested fraction. Deng et al. [108] also reported that the biogas potential from sludge was approximately 6.7 times higher than that from SLS. Yang et al. [33] stated that because of the higher levels of solids (sludge fractions), a higher HRT may be needed, which influences reactor size. However, the methane yield was still higher than that of the digestion of raw manure.

Fig. 6 shows the relative behavior of the accumulated biogas yield in the stratified fractions compared to that of raw manure (Figure A1). The supernatants exhibited a production delay when compared to the sludge fractions. However, after 1.7 d the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) had an equal yield to that of raw manure. After the initial delay presented by the supernatants (compared to the other fractions) yield rates increased, and the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) stood out in relation to other fractions, with a yield 14% higher than that of the raw manure.

According to Cao et al. [109], this behavior occurs because the methane production rate is lower than that the hydrolysis at the beginning of the process. With an improvement in biodegradability caused by SLS, the hydrolysis and acidification rates are higher and methanogenesis is reversibly inhibited by acid accumulation. Because it

is a reversible inhibition with biogas production, yield increases again (Fig. 6), as also reported by Jiang et al. [110]. 51: <header>3.2. Effect of solid-liquid separation of swine manure on BMP </header>

As concluded, the manure storage time should not be longer than 5 d therefore, the waste used was collected with a maximum of 3 d of storage. Several studies have mentioned the effects of storage on organic matter degradation and the consequent adverse outcomes regarding biogas production. Not only in the pre-treatment stage, but also in the AD stage, storage negatively affects the treatment system.

Shin et al. [88] investigated the effects of storage on GHG emissions and biogas production and noted that waste stored for 40 d emits approximately 3.7 kgCO2 eq. and reduced the effluent BMP by up to 40%. Im et al. [8] also studied the storage effect during 80 d at 35 °C on cattle manure degradation and stated that it emitted 375.1 kgCO2 eq. Tonvs and the biogas potential was reduced by 42%. It is evident that stockpiling affects the treatment system not only in terms of process efficiency, but also in terms of the environmental damage it can cause because of the emissions associated with this practice, compro- mising environmental quality.

To better understand the influence of SLS on biogas production, the mass balances of the fractions under study were initially determined (Table 4). It was possible to verify that screening generates a sludge volume of approximately 3.5 + 2.0% and settling of approximately 10.0 + 5.0%, both related to the input volume into the SLS system. Yang et al. [27] obtained similar proportions in relation to the SLS volumetric balance, with 85% of the final volume related to the supernatant and 15% to the sludge from the separation process, using a separation system composed of a hydraulic wedge-shaped sieve followed by 3 h of settling. Yang et al. [33] obtained volumetric efficiencies ranging from 10 to 30% in sludge generation in the process of SLS of swine manure through a settling column made of plexiglass.

The composition of animal manure is influenced by several factors, including animal diet, production phase, age, water consumption, and management system, which directly affect the characteristics of the final material. Consequently, a solid fraction is present, which supports the divergent values in the literature and the data observed in this study on the removal during the separation stages, both by sieving and sedi- mentation, in addition to operational differences in the SSLstage itself (e. g., mesh of sieve, sedimentation time, use of centrifuges) [32,33].

Even with the HRT changes imposed on SLS, the separation ratios were maintained, as shown in Table 4, based on the volumetric balance. However, the separation efficiency varied depending on the HRT employed for each SLS test. As the settling time increased, the concen- trations of the analyzed parameters showed a tendency to decay in the supernatants, being concentrated in the sludge, as previously discussed. As the HRT in the settling step increased, the potential also increased, even reducing the solids concentration. There was an increase in the methane production potential up to 1.2 times compared to the raw manure value. Supernatants, in general, correspond to 30-90% of the swine manure methane potential.

Amaral et al. [23] also showed that BMP was higher in the super- natant from SLS. According to the authors, the organic matter of these fractions has greater bioavailable for AD, reducing the HRT of bio- digesters. This makes it possible to treat these fractions in high-loading rate reactors (i.e., upflow anaerobic sludge blanket, UASB) [17,71]. Different sedimentation times affect the composition of the fractions present in the substrate, not only in terms of VS, but also in relation to their biodegradability, resulting changes in the C/N ratio and potentially inhibiting the process by the concentration of inhibitors in a particular fraction. Based on this, it was possible to verify the optimal operation conditions for the pre-treatment stage, considering not only the effi- ciency of the separation process but also the performance of the AD stage.

The sludge retained in the sieve exhibited the highest methane production, 51.38 + 3.11m³ N CH4 fraction, with a BMP similar to that of other analyzed fractions. In a general way, all sludge fractions had a higher BMP because of their high solids concentration (1-12%) compared to that of the supernatants (0.3-0.8%). Furthermore, the sludge corresponds to 10-60% of the biogas potential of swine manure, but with an increase of up to 14% in methane production in relation to that of raw manure.

Rico et al. [26] studied the effects of chemical treatment and screening at the technique for the separation of dairy cattle manure and found changes in the BMPs of the different fractions obtained in the SLS. The dairy manure changed from 0.320 m³ kgvs to 0.580 m³ kgvs in the separated liquid fraction and 0.258 m³ kgvs in the solid fraction. In addition to the increase in BMP, another important observation made by these authors was changes in the degradation times of the fractions. Although the liquid fraction took 21 d to reach 90% recovery of biogas, the solid fraction and raw manure took 48 and 52 d, respectively, to reach this percentage of recovery. This reinforces the discussion of the beneficial effect of SLS on the AD of animal waste, resulting in higher rates of biogas and methane production, giving higher loading capacity in digestion reactors with shorter retention times [30].

According to the results obtained in this study, the sludge fractions have the capacity to produce from 4.5 to 51.4 m³ of methane for each m³ of digested sludge. These values are approximately 7.5 times higher than that of the supernatants, which shows the capacity to produce from 1.58 to 3.45 of CH4 for each m³ of the digested fraction. Deng et al. [108] also reported that the biogas potential from sludge was approximately 6.7 times higher than that from SLS. Yang et al. [33] stated that because of the higher levels of solids (sludge fractions), a higher HRT may be needed, which influences reactor size. However, the methane yield was still higher than that of the digestion of raw manure.

Fig. 6 shows the relative behavior of the accumulated biogas yield in the stratified fractions compared to that of raw manure (Figure A1). The supernatants exhibited a production delay when compared to the sludge fractions. However, after 1.7 d the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) had an equal yield to that of raw manure. After the initial delay presented by the supernatants (compared to the other fractions) yield rates increased, and the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) stood out in relation to other fractions, with a yield 14% higher than that of the raw manure.

According to Cao et al. [109], this behavior occurs because the methane production rate is lower than that the hydrolysis at the beginning of the process. With an improvement in biodegradability caused by SLS, the hydrolysis and acidification rates are higher and methanogenesis is reversibly inhibited by acid accumulation. Because it

54: <header>3.2. Effect of solid-liquid separation of swine manure on BMP </header>

As concluded, the manure storage time should not be longer than 5 d therefore, the waste used was collected with a maximum of 3 d of storage. Several studies have mentioned the effects of storage on organic matter degradation and the consequent adverse outcomes regarding biogas production. Not only in the pre-treatment stage, but also in the AD stage, storage negatively affects the treatment system.

Shin et al. [88] investigated the effects of storage on GHG emissions and biogas production and noted that waste stored for 40 d emits approximately 3.7 kgCO2 eq. and reduced the effluent BMP by up to 40%. Im et al. [8] also studied the storage effect during 80 d at 35 °C on cattle manure degradation and stated that it emitted 375.1 kgCO2 eq. Tonvs and the biogas potential was reduced by 42%. It is evident that stockpiling affects the treatment system not only in terms of process efficiency, but also in terms of the environmental damage it can cause because of the emissions associated with this practice, compro- mising environmental quality.

To better understand the influence of SLS on biogas production, the mass balances of the fractions under study were initially determined (Table 4). It was possible to verify that screening generates a sludge volume of approximately 3.5 + 2.0% and settling of approximately 10.0 + 5.0%, both related to the input volume into the SLS system. Yang et al. [27] obtained similar proportions in relation to the SLS volumetric balance, with 85% of the final volume related to the supernatant and 15% to the sludge from the separation process, using a separation system composed of a hydraulic wedge-shaped sieve followed by 3 h of settling. Yang et al. [33] obtained volumetric efficiencies ranging from 10 to 30% in sludge generation in the process of SLS of swine manure through a settling column made of plexiglass.

The composition of animal manure is influenced by several factors, including animal diet, production phase, age, water consumption, and management system, which directly affect the characteristics of the final material. Consequently, a solid fraction is present, which supports the divergent values in the literature and the data observed in this study on the removal during the separation stages, both by sieving and sedi- mentation, in addition to operational differences in the SSLstage itself (e. g., mesh of sieve, sedimentation time, use of centrifuges) [32,33].

Even with the HRT changes imposed on SLS, the separation ratios were maintained, as shown in Table 4, based on the volumetric balance. However, the separation efficiency varied depending on the HRT employed for each SLS test. As the settling time increased, the concen- trations of the analyzed parameters showed a tendency to decay in the supernatants, being concentrated in the sludge, as previously discussed. TS removal efficiencies obtained by SLS were higher than those report

[26] studied the effects of chemical treatment and screening at the technique for the separation of dairy cattle manure and found changes in the BMPs of the different fractions obtained in the SLS. The dairy manure changed from 0.320 m³ kgvs to 0.580 m³ kgvs in the separated liquid fraction and 0.258 m³ kgvs in the solid fraction. In addition to the increase in BMP, another important observation made by these authors was changes in the degradation times of the fractions. Although the liquid fraction took 21 d to reach 90% recovery of biogas, the solid fraction and raw manure took 48 and 52 d, respectively, to reach this percentage of recovery. This reinforces the discussion of the beneficial effect of SLS on the AD of animal waste, resulting in higher rates of biogas and methane production, giving higher loading capacity in digestion reactors with shorter retention times [30].

According to the results obtained in this study, the sludge fractions have the capacity to produce from 4.5 to 51.4 m³ of methane for each m³ of digested sludge. These values are approximately 7.5 times higher than that of the supernatants, which shows the capacity to produce from 1.58 to 3.45 of CH4 for each m³ of the digested fraction. Deng et al. [108] also reported that the biogas potential from sludge was approximately 6.7 times higher than that from SLS. Yang et al. [33] stated that because of the higher levels of solids (sludge fractions), a higher HRT may be needed, which influences reactor size. However, the methane yield was still higher than that of the digestion of raw manure.

Fig. 6 shows the relative behavior of the accumulated biogas yield in the stratified fractions compared to that of raw manure (Figure A1). The supernatants exhibited a production delay when compared to the sludge fractions. However, after 1.7 d the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) had an equal yield to that of raw manure. After the initial delay presented by the supernatants (compared to the other fractions) yield rates increased, and the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) stood out in relation to other fractions, with a yield 14% higher than that of the raw manure.

According to Cao et al. [109], this behavior occurs because the methane production rate is lower than that the hydrolysis at the beginning of the process. With an improvement in biodegradability caused by SLS, the hydrolysis and acidification rates are higher and methanogenesis is reversibly inhibited by acid accumulation. Because it

57: <header>3.2. Effect of solid-liquid separation of swine manure on BMP </header>

As concluded, the manure storage time should not be longer than 5 d therefore, the waste used was collected with a maximum of 3 d of storage. Several studies have mentioned the effects of storage on organic matter degradation and the consequent adverse outcomes regarding biogas production. Not only in the pre-treatment stage, but also in the AD stage, storage negatively affects the treatment system.

Shin et al. [88] investigated the effects of storage on GHG emissions and biogas production and noted that waste stored for 40 d emits approximately 3.7 kgCO2 eq. and reduced the effluent BMP by up to 40%. Im et al. [8] also studied the storage effect during 80 d at 35 °C on cattle manure degradation and stated that it emitted 375.1 kgCO2 eq. Tonvs and the biogas potential was reduced by 42%. It is evident that stockpiling affects the treatment system not only in terms of process efficiency, but also in terms of the environmental damage it can cause because of the emissions associated with this practice, compro- mising environmental quality.

To better understand the influence of SLS on biogas production, the mass balances of the fractions under study were initially determined (Table 4). It was possible to verify that screening generates a sludge volume of approximately 3.5 + 2.0% and settling of approximately 10.0 + 5.0%, both related to the input volume into the SLS system. Yang et al. [27] obtained similar proportions in relation to the SLS volumetric balance, with 85% of the final volume related to the supernatant and 15% to the sludge from the separation process, using a separation system composed of a hydraulic wedge-shaped sieve followed by 3 h of settling. Yang et al. [33] obtained volumetric efficiencies ranging from 10 to 30% in sludge generation in the process of SLS of swine manure through a settling column made of plexiglass.

The composition of animal manure is influenced by several factors, including animal diet, production phase, age, water consumption, and management system, which directly affect the characteristics of the final material. Consequently, a solid fraction is present, which supports the divergent values in the literature and the data observed in this study on the removal during the separation stages, both by sieving and sedi- mentation, in addition to operational differences in the SSLstage itself (e. g., mesh of sieve, sedimentation time, use of centrifuges) [32,33].

Even with the HRT changes imposed on SLS, the separation ratios were maintained, as shown in Table 4, based on the volumetric balance. However, the separation efficiency varied depending on the HRT employed for each SLS test. As the settling time increased, the concen- trations of the analyzed parameters showed a tendency to decay in the supernatants, being concentrated in the sludge, as previously discussed. TS removal efficiencies obtained by SLS were higher than those report
Table 3 Swine manure characteristics (liquid fraction) after the solid-liquid separation process.

Showed no significant variation over time according to Tukey test (p 0.05). Note: Different lowercase letters indicate different means between days for each parameter inside the treatments and different uppercase letters indicate different means between treatments for each day and parameter, according to Tukey test, p < 0.05.

by Zhang et al. [34], which achieved 40% after 120 min of SLS of swine wastes by settling. This difference can be explained by the storage time because the authors stocked the manure for 2 months, compromising the SLS.

After the SLS balances were determined, BMP tests were performed to compare the effect of SLS on AD. As presented by Jingura and Kamusoko [107], BMP tests are the basis for determining the perfor- mance of the AD process, being a high reliability and widely used method. These tests resulted in a higher methane yield value in the su- pernatant from settling with a 30 min HRT, as shown in Table 5. As the HRT in the settling step increased, the potential also increased, even reducing the solids concentration. There was an increase in the methane production potential up to 1.2 times compared to the raw manure value. Supernatants, in general, correspond to 30-90% of the swine manure methane potential.

Amaral et al. [23] also showed that BMP was higher in the super- natant from SLS. According to the authors, the organic matter of these fractions has greater bioavailable for AD, reducing the HRT of bio- digesters. This makes it possible to treat these fractions in high-loading rate reactors (i.e., upflow anaerobic sludge blanket, UASB) [17,71]. Different sedimentation times affect the composition of the fractions present in the substrate, not only in terms of VS, but also in relation to their biodegradability, resulting changes in the C/N ratio and potentially inhibiting the process by the concentration of inhibitors in a particular fraction. Based on this, it was possible to verify the optimal operation conditions for the pre-treatment stage, considering not only the effi- ciency of the separation process but also the performance of the AD stage.

The sludge retained in the sieve exhibited the highest methane production, 51.38 + 3.11m³ N CH4 fraction, with a BMP similar to that of other analyzed fractions. In a general way, all sludge fractions had a higher BMP because of their high solids concentration (1-12%) compared to that of the supernatants (0.3-0.8%). Furthermore, the sludge corresponds to 10-60% of the biogas potential of swine manure, but with an increase of up to 14% in methane production in relation to that of raw manure.

The supernatants exhibited a production delay when compared to the sludge fractions. However, after 1.7 d the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) had an equal yield to that of raw manure. After the initial delay presented by the supernatants (compared to the other fractions) yield rates increased, and the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) stood out in relation to other fractions, with a yield 14% higher than that of the raw manure.

According to Cao et al. [109], this behavior occurs because the methane production rate is lower than that the hydrolysis at the beginning of the process. With an improvement in biodegradability caused by SLS, the hydrolysis and acidification rates are higher and methanogenesis is reversibly inhibited by acid accumulation. Because it

resulting from SLS of swine manure, whereas for the solid fraction and the raw manure, the peak production ranged between 3 and 9 d. 60: <header>3.2. Effect of solid-liquid separation of swine manure on BMP </header>

As concluded, the manure storage time should not be longer than 5 d therefore, the waste used was collected with a maximum of 3 d of storage. Several studies have mentioned the effects of storage on organic matter degradation and the consequent adverse outcomes regarding biogas production. Not only in the pre-treatment stage, but also in the AD stage, storage negatively affects the treatment system.

Shin et al. [88] investigated the effects of storage on GHG emissions and biogas production and noted that waste stored for 40 d emits approximately 3.7 kgCO2 eq. and reduced the effluent BMP by up to 40%. Im et al. [8] also studied the storage effect during 80 d at 35 °C on cattle manure degradation and stated that it emitted 375.1 kgCO2 eq. Tonvs and the biogas potential was reduced by 42%. It is evident that stockpiling affects the treatment system not only in terms of process efficiency, but also in terms of the environmental damage it can cause because of the emissions associated with this practice, compro- mising environmental quality.

To better understand the influence of SLS on biogas production, the mass balances of the fractions under study were initially determined (Table 4). It was possible to verify that screening generates a sludge volume of approximately 3.5 + 2.0% and settling of approximately 10.0 + 5.0%, both related to the input volume into the SLS system. Yang et al. [27] obtained similar proportions in relation to the SLS volumetric balance, with 85% of the final volume related to the supernatant and 15% to the sludge from the separation process, using a separation system composed of a hydraulic wedge-shaped sieve followed by 3 h of settling. Yang et al. [33] obtained volumetric efficiencies ranging from 10 to 30% in sludge generation in the process of SLS of swine manure through a settling column made of plexiglass.

The composition of animal manure is influenced by several factors, including animal diet, production phase, age, water consumption, and management system, which directly affect the characteristics of the final material. Consequently, a solid fraction is present, which supports the divergent values in the literature and the data observed in this study on the removal during the separation stages, both by sieving and sedi- mentation, in addition to operational differences in the SSLstage itself (e. g., mesh of sieve, sedimentation time, use of centrifuges) [32,33].

Even with the HRT changes imposed on SLS, the separation ratios were maintained, as shown in Table 4, based on the volumetric balance. However, the separation efficiency varied depending on the HRT employed for each SLS test. As the settling time increased, the concen- trations of the analyzed parameters showed a tendency to decay in the supernatants, being concentrated in the sludge, as previously discussed. TS removal efficiencies obtained by SLS were higher than those report
Table 3 Swine manure characteristics (liquid fraction) after the solid-liquid separation process.

Showed no significant variation over time according to Tukey test (p 0.05). Note: Different lowercase letters indicate different means between days for each parameter inside the treatments and different uppercase letters indicate different means between treatments for each day and parameter, according to Tukey test, p < 0.05.

by Zhang et al. [34], which achieved 40% after 120 min of SLS of swine wastes by settling. This difference can be explained by the storage time because the authors stocked the manure for 2 months, compromising the SLS.

After the SLS balances were determined, BMP tests were performed to compare the effect of SLS on AD. As presented by Jingura and Kamusoko [107], BMP tests are the basis for determining the perfor- mance of the AD process, being a high reliability and widely used method. These tests resulted in a higher methane yield value in the su- pernatant from settling with a 30 min HRT, as shown in Table 5. As the HRT in the settling step increased, the potential also increased, even reducing the solids concentration. There was an increase in the methane production potential up to 1.2 times compared to the raw manure value. Supernatants, in general, correspond to 30-90% of the swine manure methane potential.

Amaral et al. [23] also showed that BMP was higher in the super- natant from SLS. According to the authors, the organic matter of these fractions has greater bioavailable for AD, reducing the HRT of bio- digesters. This makes it possible to treat these fractions in high-loading rate reactors (i.e., upflow anaerobic sludge blanket, UASB) [17,71]. Different sedimentation times affect the composition of the fractions present in the substrate, not only in terms of VS, but also in relation to their biodegradability, resulting changes in the C/N ratio and potentially inhibiting the process by the concentration of inhibitors in a particular fraction. Based on this, it was possible to verify the optimal operation conditions for the pre-treatment stage, considering not only the effi- ciency of the separation process but also the performance of the AD stage.

The sludge retained in the sieve exhibited the highest methane production, 51.38 + 3.11m³ N CH4 fraction, with a BMP similar to that of other analyzed fractions. In a general way, all sludge fractions had a higher BMP because of their high solids concentration (1-12%) compared to that of the supernatants (0.3-0.8%). Furthermore, the sludge corresponds to 10-60% of the biogas potential of swine manure, but with an increase of up to 14% in methane production in relation to that of raw manure.

Rico et al. [26] studied the effects of chemical treatment and screening at the technique for the separation of dairy cattle manure and found changes in the BMPs of the different fractions obtained in the SLS. The dairy manure changed from 0.320 m³ kgvs to 0.580 m³ kgvs in the separated liquid fraction and 0.258 m³ kgvs in the solid fraction. In addition to the increase in BMP, another important observation made by these authors was changes in the degradation times of the fractions. Although the liquid fraction took 21 d to reach 90% recovery of biogas, the solid fraction and raw manure took 48 and 52 d, respectively, to reach this percentage of recovery. This reinforces the discussion of the beneficial effect of SLS on the AD of animal waste, resulting in higher rates of biogas and methane production, giving higher loading capacity in digestion reactors with shorter retention times [30].

According to the results obtained in this study, the sludge fractions have the capacity to produce from 4.5 to 51.4 m³ of methane for each m³ of digested sludge. These values are approximately 7.5 times higher than that of the supernatants, which shows the capacity to produce from 1.58 to 3.45 of CH4 for each m³ of the digested fraction. Deng et al. [108] also reported that the biogas potential from sludge was approximately 6.7 times higher than that from SLS. Yang et al. [33] stated that because of the higher levels of solids (sludge fractions), a higher HRT may be needed, which influences reactor size. However, the methane yield was still higher than that of the digestion of raw manure.

Fig. 6 shows the relative behavior of the accumulated biogas yield in the stratified fractions compared to that of raw manure (Figure A1). The supernatants exhibited a production delay when compared to the sludge fractions. However, after 1.7 d the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) had an equal yield to that of raw manure. After the initial delay presented by the supernatants (compared to the other fractions) yield rates increased, and the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) stood out in relation to other fractions, with a yield 14% higher than that of the raw manure.

According to Cao et al. [109], this behavior occurs because the methane production rate is lower than that the hydrolysis at the beginning of the process. With an improvement in biodegradability caused by SLS, the hydrolysis and acidification rates are higher and methanogenesis is reversibly inhibited by acid accumulation. Because it

M2 (Raw manure): TOC: 3952 + 633 mg L-1: TAN:1124.9 + 238.5 mgN L TS: 12.45 + 0.3 mg VS: 8.9 0.3 mg L-¹ a C/C (Mass/M2 Mass).

is a reversible inhibition with biogas production, yield increases again (Fig. 6), as also reported by Jiang et al. [110]. The modified Gompertz model was used as a support for the kinetic analysis of biogas production on the stratified swine manure samples (Table 6). The correlation co- efficient (R2) of each treatment was higher than 99.70 + 0.01, indicating that the proposed model fit the data and there was no significant dif- ference between the expected biogas yield and that obtained experimentally.

According to that observed on day 5.3 + 0.2, the maximum rate of biogas production exhibited the lowest value for the sludge retained in the sieve (47.5 + 2.7 mLN biogas gvs d-¹ and higher values were observed for the supernatant from screening (60.8 + biogas d-¹ and the settled supernatant (HRT 30 min) (60.2 + mLN biogas gvs d-¹. Yang et al. [27] found that daily methane production peaked on day 3 for the SLS fractions, whereas for the pig slurry fraction peak production was reached on day 4 only. Amaral et al. [23] obtained the maximum methane production on day 1 for most of the liquid fractions
Table 5
Methane production potential for the studied fractions.
Note: Different letters means statistically significant difference by Tukey test (p 0.05).

resulting from SLS of swine manure, whereas for the solid fraction and the raw manure, the peak production ranged between 3 and 9 d. These rates were related to the biodegradability and availability of organic matter and indicated that SLS contributed to biogas improvement, with differences from the literature being inherent to the substrate [111].
63: <header>3.3. Energy production potential and GHG mitigation </header>

Considering the proposed scenarios in accordance with the results discussed above, Table 7 shows the bioenergy potential production of the three hypotheses. Generally, a hypothetical farm with 5000 breeding females and a daily average effluent production of 111.9 m³ had a maximum energy potential of 1161.1 + 34.9 kWh d-¹.

Scenario 2 stood out in terms of energy production, with values 1.2 times higher than that the other scenarios. The adoption of strategies, such as those proposed in Scenarios 2 and 3, which separated the treatment according to the waste solids content, improved the perfor- mance of the CLB, increasing its efficiency and useful life. These oper- ational advantages are related to the SLS that promotes better use of organic matter as well as lower solids load and HRT for the reactor. Because of this, in the medium/long term, the energy gain would offset the investment in yet another unit process (in this case, SLS) [27,71].

Atelge et al. (2020) [30] emphasized that waste pre-treatment is an important step prior to AD. It allows better solid degradation, improves treatment performance, and consequently, lowers costs of the installa- tion and maintenance of the systems. The AD of swine waste is be an adequate alternative to mitigate GHG emissions resulting from manure storage [4]. For the theoretical scenarios, the emission reductions would be approximately 280 TonCO2 eq year which is a low value compared to national indicators.
In Brazil, there are 334 plants for animal waste treatment through AD, which implements the energy use of biogas. Together, they have an

annual capacity to produce 164.67 X 106 m³ of methane, annually mitigating the emission of 153.8 X 106 TonCO2 eq. However, considering the amount of waste generated by the energy potential, these data still represent a small portion [112].

According to Freitas et al. [36], biogas from biomass represents 0.08% of the Brazilian energy matrix. In total, animal waste corresponds to only 1.73%. Tursi [16] pointed out that biomass is a valuable source to supply worldwide energy needs, with the capacity to suppress more than 80 times the annual consumption of energy in the world. That it is not only present in animal waste, but also in different areas, with distinct energy potentials and composition for each waste.

The emergence of alternative energy sources to replace fossil fuels has been discussed worldwide, and several countries have been invest- ing in the use of waste as an energy source [14]. In this sense, and ac- cording to the data throughout the present research, animal manure is an excellent alternative to complement the Brazilian energy matrix, because it allows the management and reduces environmental impacts and GHG emissions [77,113].

These oper- ational advantages are related to the SLS that promotes better use of organic matter as well as lower solids load and HRT for the reactor. Because of this, in the medium/long term, the energy gain would offset the investment in yet another unit process (in this case, SLS) [27,71].

annual capacity to produce 164.67 X 106 m³ of methane, annually mitigating the emission of 153.8 X 106 TonCO2 eq. However, considering the amount of waste generated by the energy potential, these data still represent a small portion [112].

According to Freitas et al. [36], biogas from biomass represents 0.08% of the Brazilian energy matrix. In total, animal waste corresponds to only 1.73%. Tursi [16] pointed out that biomass is a valuable source to supply worldwide energy needs, with the capacity to suppress more than 80 times the annual consumption of energy in the world. That it is not only present in animal waste, but also in different areas, with distinct energy potentials and composition for each waste.

The emergence of alternative energy sources to replace fossil fuels has been discussed worldwide, and several countries have been invest- ing in the use of waste as an energy source [14]. In this sense, and ac- cording to the data throughout the present research, animal manure is an excellent alternative to complement the Brazilian energy matrix, because it allows the management and reduces environmental impacts and GHG emissions [77,113].
76: <header>3.4. Life-cycle impact assessment results of analyzed studies </header>

The baseline presented the greatest environmental impact compared to Scenarios 1 and 2. These higher impacts were related to the swine manure storage for a long period (120 d), which promotes GHG emis- sions and nutrient losses by leaching into soil and water.

The "climate change, short term" category is the one that showed the greatest negative influence of manure storage. As the storage time decreased, there was a reduction of up to 50% of the impacts caused by manure storage in this midpoint category (Fig. 7). Brockmann et al. [114] assessed the effects of manure storage and application in the soil and found that storage promoted an impact four times greater than the scenario with effluent treatment and disposal (nitrification and
denitrification, followed by soil application). This shows that treatment systems that aim to reduce manure storage time contribute to the reduction of impacts associated with management systems.

The positive effects observed in the baseline are attributed to nutrient recovery categories, because of the mitigation of environmental damage compared to the use of chemical fertilizers. Several studies have found the same positive effects associated with replacing the use of mineral fertilizers with the effects associated with climate change. Lansche and Müller [115] verified a reduction of up to 10 gCO eq. Brockmann et al. [114] observed a reduction of approximately 20% in emissions. Ramirez-Islas et al. [10] found a benefit of 50 gCO2 eq attributed to replacement of chemical fertilizers. The impacts generated for photochemical oxidant formation were mitigated by the environ- mental gains from the substitution products. The most harmful stage is related to the operation of the treatment plants, which supported the most favorable values for the baseline scenario.

For freshwater eutrophication and acidification categories, the sce- narios did not differ in terms of impact. In terms of While terrestrial acidification, marine eutrophication, and freshwater ecotoxicity, the variation among the scenarios was low (2-5%). For human toxicity (cancer and non-cancer related), the discrepancies were between 5% and 20%. In general, for these categories, the transportation and fertil- ization stages negatively contributed to the impacts.

Transportation and fertilization promote the release of higher amounts of gases into the atmosphere, such as ammonia and other nitrogenous compounds. This occurrence is associated with manure spreading, which impacts air, water, and soil. Phosphorous, ammonia, nitrate, copper, and zinc are the main factors responsible for eutrophi- cation, soil acidification, and environmental toxicity [10,19,116,117]. According to the LCA of the fertilization process verified by Corbala-Robles et al. [116], the disposal of effluents in the field gener- ates relevant environmental impacts. It contributes to eutrophication,
acidification, and toxicity processes, similar to the results found in the present study (Fig. 7).

Despite the impacts associated with the operational stage of the treatment plants, Scenarios 1 and 2 stood out in relation to the baseline, with the advantages related to nutrient recovery, energy, and heat production. Scenario 2 exhibited the best environmental results. Thus, the performance of the system in energy recovery was higher, which may be attributed to the lower manure storage time, SLS techniques, and AD in both phases (liquid and solid). Thus, the biogas yield was higher and in relation to Scenario 1, Scenario 2 presented an increase up to 1.3 times. According to Dennehy et al. [9], suitable manure management of each fraction (solid and liquid) is one of the key elements to mitigate GHG emissions in a swine treatment installation, as evidenced in the present study. Additionally, energy recovery is attractive from an eco- nomic viewpoint, reducing the cost of swine production units.

The pre-treatment technologies presented promising results regarding the treatment and energy recovery of the system, emphasizing the importance of handling the waste during the storage stage, which has a direct effect on the emission rates of the systems. Because there is an uncertainty associated with the emission rates, the sensitivity anal- ysis performed to investigate how an increase or decrease of 15% and 25% in the emissions during the storage stage would affect the perfor- mance of the scenarios (Figure A2).

The highest sensitivity was observed for the climate change category, where both the 25% reduction and increase in emission rates resulted in an increase or reduction of 27.31%, 30.82%, and 31.51%, respectively in the baseline, Scenario 1, and Scenario 2. For 15% variation in emis- sion rates, there was an increase or decrease in impacts by 16.35%, 18.57%, and 18.64% in the baseline, Scenario 1, and Scenario 2, respectively. Photochemical oxidant formation was the second most sensitive category, with an increase or decrease in the impact by 14.90% for a 25% variation in emissions and an increase or decrease in the impact by 8.92% for the 15% variation, at baseline.

The other categories presented lower sensitivities, for freshwater acidification, the baseline scenario presented similar variation either in the increase or decrease of emissions from storage for the two percent- ages. An impact of 5.44% occurred given a variation of 25% in emissions and by 3.63% for 15% variation in emissions. In Scenario 2 for the same category, the variation in emission rates presented different impacts, related to the increase, or decrease in rates. A 25% reduction in emis- sions from storage resulted in a reduction of 3.13% in the effect, whereas an increase of 25% in emission rates resulted in an increase of 2.73% in the impact of this category. For the marine eutrophication category, Scenario 1, exhibited a 4.51% reduction in the category impact with a 25% reduction in emissions, whereas an increase resulted in a 3.94% increase in impacts. For the terrestrial acidification category, 15% emission rate variations resulted in a 1.12% reduction and 2.62% in- crease for Scenario 2, whereas the 25% variation in Scenario 2 resulted in a 3.68% increase or reduction in the impact for the terrestrial acidi- fication category.

These data corroborated those observed by Duan et al. [19], who also found the highest sensitivity associated with the climate change cate- gory by changing emissions rates by 25%. Cherubini et al. [77], emphasized that in LCA modeling, the greatest uncertainties are related to emission estimates in waste management because of the difficulties regarding inaccurate measurements and estimates.

These results demonstrated the importance of management related to waste storage, because the emission rates are directly related to the storage time varying according to the characteristics of the waste. This strongly reflected the impact on climate change.

These data corroborated those observed by Duan et al. [19], who also found the highest sensitivity associated with the climate change cate- gory by changing emissions rates by 25%. Cherubini et al. [77], emphasized that in LCA modeling, the greatest uncertainties are related to emission estimates in waste management because of the difficulties regarding inaccurate measurements and estimates.

These results demonstrated the importance of management related to waste storage, because the emission rates are directly related to the storage time varying according to the characteristics of the waste. This strongly reflected the impact on climate change.
81: <header>3.4. Life-cycle impact assessment results of analyzed studies </header>

The baseline presented the greatest environmental impact compared to Scenarios 1 and 2. These higher impacts were related to the swine manure storage for a long period (120 d), which promotes GHG emis- sions and nutrient losses by leaching into soil and water.

The "climate change, short term" category is the one that showed the greatest negative influence of manure storage. As the storage time decreased, there was a reduction of up to 50% of the impacts caused by manure storage in this midpoint category (Fig. 7). Brockmann et al. [114] assessed the effects of manure storage and application in the soil and found that storage promoted an impact four times greater than the scenario with effluent treatment and disposal (nitrification and
denitrification, followed by soil application). This shows that treatment systems that aim to reduce manure storage time contribute to the reduction of impacts associated with management systems.

The positive effects observed in the baseline are attributed to nutrient recovery categories, because of the mitigation of environmental damage compared to the use of chemical fertilizers. Several studies have found the same positive effects associated with replacing the use of mineral fertilizers with the effects associated with climate change. Lansche and Müller [115] verified a reduction of up to 10 gCO eq. Brockmann et al. [114] observed a reduction of approximately 20% in emissions. Ramirez-Islas et al. [10] found a benefit of 50 gCO2 eq attributed to replacement of chemical fertilizers. The impacts generated for photochemical oxidant formation were mitigated by the environ- mental gains from the substitution products. The most harmful stage is related to the operation of the treatment plants, which supported the most favorable values for the baseline scenario.

For freshwater eutrophication and acidification categories, the sce- narios did not differ in terms of impact. In terms of While terrestrial acidification, marine eutrophication, and freshwater ecotoxicity, the variation among the scenarios was low (2-5%). For human toxicity (cancer and non-cancer related), the discrepancies were between 5% and 20%. In general, for these categories, the transportation and fertil- ization stages negatively contributed to the impacts.

Transportation and fertilization promote the release of higher amounts of gases into the atmosphere, such as ammonia and other nitrogenous compounds. This occurrence is associated with manure spreading, which impacts air, water, and soil. Phosphorous, ammonia, nitrate, copper, and zinc are the main factors responsible for eutrophi- cation, soil acidification, and environmental toxicity [10,19,116,117]. According to the LCA of the fertilization process verified by Corbala-Robles et al. [116], the disposal of effluents in the field gener- ates relevant environmental impacts. It contributes to eutrophication,
acidification, and toxicity processes, similar to the results found in the present study (Fig. 7).

Despite the impacts associated with the operational stage of the treatment plants, Scenarios 1 and 2 stood out in relation to the baseline, with the advantages related to nutrient recovery, energy, and heat production. Scenario 2 exhibited the best environmental results. Thus, the performance of the system in energy recovery was higher, which may be attributed to the lower manure storage time, SLS techniques, and AD in both phases (liquid and solid). Thus, the biogas yield was higher and in relation to Scenario 1, Scenario 2 presented an increase up to 1.3 times. According to Dennehy et al. [9], suitable manure management of each fraction (solid and liquid) is one of the key elements to mitigate GHG emissions in a swine treatment installation, as evidenced in the present study. Additionally, energy recovery is attractive from an eco- nomic viewpoint, reducing the cost of swine production units.

The pre-treatment technologies presented promising results regarding the treatment and energy recovery of the system, emphasizing the importance of handling the waste during the storage stage, which has a direct effect on the emission rates of the systems. Because there is an uncertainty associated with the emission rates, the sensitivity anal- ysis performed to investigate how an increase or decrease of 15% and 25% in the emissions during the storage stage would affect the perfor- mance of the scenarios (Figure A2).

The highest sensitivity was observed for the climate change category, where both the 25% reduction and increase in emission rates resulted in an increase or reduction of 27.31%, 30.82%, and 31.51%, respectively in the baseline, Scenario 1, and Scenario 2. For 15% variation in emis- sion rates, there was an increase or decrease in impacts by 16.35%, 18.57%, and 18.64% in the baseline, Scenario 1, and Scenario 2, respectively. Photochemical oxidant formation was the second most sensitive category, with an increase or decrease in the impact by 14.90% for a 25% variation in emissions and an increase or decrease in the impact by 8.92% for the 15% variation, at baseline.

The other categories presented lower sensitivities, for freshwater acidification, the baseline scenario presented similar variation either in the increase or decrease of emissions from storage for the two percent- ages. An impact of 5.44% occurred given a variation of 25% in emissions and by 3.63% for 15% variation in emissions. In Scenario 2 for the same category, the variation in emission rates presented different impacts, related to the increase, or decrease in rates. A 25% reduction in emis- sions from storage resulted in a reduction of 3.13% in the effect, whereas an increase of 25% in emission rates resulted in an increase of 2.73% in the impact of this category. For the marine eutrophication category, Scenario 1, exhibited a 4.51% reduction in the category impact with a 25% reduction in emissions, whereas an increase resulted in a 3.94% increase in impacts. For the terrestrial acidification category, 15% emission rate variations resulted in a 1.12% reduction and 2.62% in- crease for Scenario 2, whereas the 25% variation in Scenario 2 resulted in a 3.68% increase or reduction in the impact for the terrestrial acidi- fication category.

These data corroborated those observed by Duan et al. [19], who also found the highest sensitivity associated with the climate change cate- gory by changing emissions rates by 25%. Cherubini et al. [77], emphasized that in LCA modeling, the greatest uncertainties are related to emission estimates in waste management because of the difficulties regarding inaccurate measurements and estimates.

These results demonstrated the importance of management related to waste storage, because the emission rates are directly related to the storage time varying according to the characteristics of the waste. This strongly reflected the impact on climate change.
