83: <header>3.5. Future perspectives and practical implications of this study </header>

Because of global activities and prospects of carbon emissions reduction, the feasibility of the swine manure production in a view of "carbon-neutral" operation is being globally emphasized [118]. AD of
swine manure is a conventional biological process used to convert organic matter into energy (methane-rich biogas) and fertilizer.

The development of renewable energy from animal manure is conducive to improving the energy structure worldwide, reducing car- bon emissions, and achieving the purpose of protecting the ecological environment. Therefore, because there is an urgent need to develop an alternative to animal manure management, renewable power genera- tion from it is an approach for recycling waste. However, the current technologies used for animal waste management (AD) needs to be re- evaluated. Improving technologies and optimizing agricultural prac- tices are essential for the sustainable development of renewable power generation from manure management. Based on the results of this study, some suggestions regarding manure storage time and SLS techniques before AD, may be good alternatives to maximize the use of waste and energy production.

In response to the above SLS technique, the swine manure storage time also has implications for the feasibility of yield treatment. Based on the results, the key for a treatment system to maximize energy produc- tion, must be a perfect balance between manure storage time, the SLS technique, and AD. Moreover, beyond SLS methodologies, some research is needed to determine the best technique, which may vary according to the type of waste in question (i.e., solids, content, and biodegradability of organic matter).

Under the above circumstances, the volume of sludge also changed according to the manure storage time. This is related to swine farms, because sludge has limited applications in AD. Thus, the present assessment shows that the efficiency and sustainability of swine manure management is critically dependent on an integrated understanding of the underlying component interactions, which also impacts the LCAs. Credits for the whole farm system benefit from the aforementioned sit- uation of the entire treatment: greenhouse gas emissions and farm environmental effects are minimized with the combination of the ideal manure storage time, SLS, and AD. From a global perspective, these methods also maximize biogas production and yield.

Although the results are promising, there are many factors that in- fluence the systems under study. According to Aghbashlo et al. [119], AD plant designs depend on more advanced engineering analyses, not only on the design, but also on the operations and processes involved [120]. In addition to the LCA approach, studies of the processes, ener- getic and/or thermodynamic, by means of exergy and exergoenvir- onmental analyses, are important tools for determining the environmental sustainability of AD projects [121,122].

Looking toward future projections and applicability based on the implemented results, before the advanced wastewater-based swine manure biogas production technologies, more research is needed to: (i) better understand the mechanisms of swine manure organic matter degradation/removal according to storage time and/or AD; (ii) improve the capabilities of SLS technologies for different types of wastewater with sludge and solid content; (iii) optimize the parameters of SLS; (iv) optimize the environmental parameters and techniques to combine the best conditions for storage time, SLS, and AD directly on swine farms; (v) develop efficient and cost-effective SLS methods; (vi) integrate the biorefinery processes and concepts for economical production of biogas from swine manure; (vii) conduct techno-economic assessment to determine the economic viability of the aforementioned set; (viii) analyze LCA of other environmental impacts of the swine farms; and (ix) perform more in-depth investigations using advanced thermodynamic methods, such as exergy, exergoeconomic, and exergoenvironmental analyses. All these will guide the system to integrate and innovate farms in a more sustainable, economically viable, and environmentally friendly way. The profits for the producer are in the maximization of energy generation and biofertilizer quality. It all adds to the sustain- ability tripod and circular economy concepts.

87: In this work it was concluded that:

<list>(i) The storage time had a significant influence on the SLS by screening. After 5 d of storage, the separation was drastically compromised, the sludge volume generated increased accord- ingly and the performance of the treatment system was compromised; 
(ii) Al though chemical flocculation showed greater performance that settling, both methodologies are satisfactory for manure treatment by subsequent AD; 
(iii) The fractions from SLS have different biogas and methane yields, and such pretreatment improves the performance of a manure treatment system; 
(vi) The supernatant from SLS by screening and 30 min of settling provided the highest methane potential, with the capacity to generate up to 4.5 kWh of electricity/m3 of raw manure, miti- gating GHG emissions, and managing a large volume of animal wastes; 
(vii) According to the LCA, the GHG emissions and environmental impacts of a manure treatment plant can be reduced if the system works with low effluent storage time, SLS techniques, and AD of liquid and solid phases separately.</list>
